$\widehat { \gamma } = 4 . 6 0$ (for $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ vs. $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$

Ratio of survival times:
$\frac { 5 5 . 3 } { 1 2 . 0 } = \frac { 2 7 . 6 } { 6 . 0 } = \frac { 1 1 . 5 } { 2 . 5 } = 4 . 6 0$

Effect of treatment:

· Stretches survival by a factor of
4.6

· Interpretation of $\gamma$ has intuitive
appeal

On the left is Stata output from the AFT form of
the exponential model with TRT as the only
predictor. The estimate of the coefficient for
TRT is 1.527 with a standard error of 0.398.
An estimate of the acceleration factor for treat-
ment is $\widehat { \gamma } = \exp \left( 1 . 5 2 7 \right) = 4 . 6 0 .$ A 95% confi-
dence interval for $\gamma$ is calculated as exp[1.527
$\pm 1 . 9 6 \left( 0 . 3 9 8 \right)$ yielding a CI of (2.11, 10.05).

The parameter estimates can be used to esti-
mate the time t to any value of $S \left( t \right) = q .$ The
table on the left lists the estimated time (in
weeks) for the first, second (median), and
third quartiles of $\mathrm { S } \left( t \right)$ using the expression for
$\widehat { t }$ shown above for both the treated and placebo
groups. In this example, survival time is the
time to remission for leukemia patients.

The ratio of survival times for each row in the
table comparing $\mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s } .$ $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ is 4.60,
which not coincidently is the estimate of the
acceleration factor (see left). The estimated
acceleration factor suggests that the experi-
mental treatment is effective for delaying
remission by stretching survival time by a fac-
tor of 4.60. Although the hazard ratio is a
more familiar measure of association for
health scientists, the acceleration factor has
an intuitive appeal, particularly for describing
the efficacy of a treatment on survival.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: V. Exponential Example Revisited 303" -->

HR and $\gamma$ are reciprocals in expo-
nential models:

$$\widehat { H R } \left( \mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s . } 0 \right) = \exp \left( - 1 . 5 2 7 \right)$$
$$= 0 . 2 2$$
$$\widehat { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s . } 0 \right) = \exp \left( 1 . 5 2 7 \right)$$
$$= 4 . 6 0$$

In general

$\gamma > 1 \Rightarrow \text { exposure benefi }$
survival

$\mathrm { H R } > 1 \Rightarrow \mathrm { e x p o s u r e }$ harmful to
survival

$\gamma > 1 \Rightarrow \mathrm { e x p o s u r e } \mathrm { h a r m f u l }$ to
survival

$H R < 1 \Rightarrow$ exposure benefits
survival

$\gamma = \mathrm { H R } = 1 \Rightarrow \mathrm { n o }$ effect from
exposure

Exponential PH and AFT models:

· Same model

· Different parameterization

· Same estimates for

o Survival function

o Hazard function

o Median survival

Recall from Section III that the hazard ratio
for the effect of treatment was estimated
at $\exp \left( - 1 . 5 2 7 \right) = 0 . 2 2$ using the PH form of
the exponential model. This result illustrates
a key property of the exponential model: the
corresponding acceleration factor and hazards
ratio (e.g., $\mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s } .$ TRT = 0) are reciprocals
of each other. This property is unique to the
exponential model. What can be generalized,
however, is that an acceleration factor
greater than one for the effect of an expo-
sure implies that being exposed (i.e., $\mathrm { T R T } =$
1\) is beneficial to survival whereas a hazard
ratio greater than one implies being exposed
is harmful to survival (and vice versa).

Although the exponential PH and AFT models
focus on different underlying assumptions,
they are in fact the same model. The only
difference is in their parameterization. The
resulting estimates for the survival function,
hazard function, and median survival do
not differ between these models (see Practice
Exercises 6 and 7).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="304 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

For those experienced with Poisson
regression:

Exponential and Poisson models

· Assume a constant rate

· Different data structure

o Poisson - aggregate counts

o Exponential - individual
level

· Use different outcomes

o Poisson - number of cases

o Exponential - time to $\mathrm { e v e n } t$

· Yield equivalent parameter
estimates

o With same data and same
covariates in the model

For those who have experience with Poisson
regression, there is a close connection between
the exponential and Poisson models. Both dis-
tributions assume an underlying constant
rate. In fact, if the data are structured such
that all the cases and the total time at risk are
aggregated for each pattern of covariates (e.g.,
$\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ and $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ and the $\log$ of the
corresponding person-time at risk is used as
an offset, then a Poisson model will yield equiv-
alent parameter estimates as the exponential
PH model. The difference is that the random
outcome for the Poisson model is the count of
events over a fixed amount of time at risk and
the random outcome for the exponential model
is the time (at risk) to event.

Exponential model is special case
of Weibull model

We continue with the remission data example
and present the more general Weibull model,
which includes the exponential model as a spe-
cial case. In the next section we also show a
graphical approach for evaluating the appro-
priateness of the Weibull (and thus also the
exponential) model.


## VI. Weibull Example

Weibull Model:

Hazard function: $h \left( t \right) = 2 p t ^ { p - 1 }$
(where $p > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

$p$ is a shape parameter

· $p > 1 \quad h a z a r d \quad i n c r e a s e s \quad o v e r$
time

· $p = 1 \quad c o n s t a n t \quad h a z a r d$
(exponential model)

· $p < 1$ hazard decreases over
time

Additional shape parameter offers
greater flexibility

The Weibull model is the most widely used
parametric survival model. Its hazard function
is $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \lambda p t ^ { p - 1 } ,$ where $p$ and $\lambda > 0 .$ As with the
exponential model, $\lambda$ will be reparameterized
with regression coefficients. The additional
parameter $p$ is called a shape parameter and
determines the shape of the hazard function.
If $p > 1$ then the hazard increases as time
increases. $\mathrm { I f } p = 1$ then the hazard is constant
and the Weibull model reduces to the exponen-
tial model $\left( \mathrm { h } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) = \widehat { } \lambda \right) .$ If $p < 1$ then the hazard
decreases over time. The addition of this shape
parameter gives the Weibull model greater
flexibility than the exponential model, yet the
hazard function remains relatively simple (basi-
cally a scaling of t raised to some fixed power).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: VI. Weibull Example 305" -->

Unique property for Weibull model
$\mathrm { A F T } \Rightarrow \mathrm { P H }$ and $\mathrm { P H } \Rightarrow \mathrm { A F T }$
Holds if $p$ is fixed

HR vs. AFT

Hazard ratio => Comparison of rates

Acceleration $\mathrm { f a c t o r } \Rightarrow \mathrm { E f f e c t }$ on
survival

Useful Weibull property:

· $\ln \left[ - \ln S \left( t \right) \right]$ is linear with $\ln \left( t \right)$

· Enables graphical evaluation
using KM survival estimates

Linearity of $\ln \left( t \right)$

$$S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t ^ { p } \right)$$

$\Rightarrow \ln \left[ - \ln S \left( t \right) \right] = \ln \left( \lambda \right) + p \ln \left( t \right)$
1
1

$\mathrm { I n t e r c e p t } = \ln \left( \lambda \right) ,$ $S l o p e = p$

Remission data: evaluate Weibull
assumption for $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ and $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$


<figure>
<figcaption>$\ln \left[ - \ln \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right]$ plotted against $\ln \left( t \right)$</figcaption>

2

$. T R T = 0 + + + T R T = 1$

1

0

-1

-2

-3

0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0

$\mathrm { L o g }$ of time

</figure>


The Weibull model has the property that if the
AFT assumption holds then the PH assump-
tion also holds (and vice versa). This property
is unique to the Weibull model (Cox and
Oakes, 1984) and holds if $p$ does not vary over
different levels of covariates. The PH assump-
tion allows for the estimation of a hazard ratio
enabling a comparison of rates among differ-
ent populations. The AFT assumption allows
for the estimation of an acceleration factor,
which can describe the direct effect of an expo-
sure on survival time.

The Weibull model also has another key prop-
erty: the $\log \left( - \log \right)$ of S(t) is linear with the $\log$
of time. This allows a graphical evaluation of
the appropriateness of a Weibull model by plot-
ting the log negative $\log \mathrm { o f }$ the Kaplan-Meier
survival estimates against the log of time.

To see this linear relationship: start with the
Weibull survival function $S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t ^ { p } \right) ,$ take
the $\log$ of $S \left( t \right) ,$ multiply by negative one, and
take the $\log$ again (see left). For the Weibull
distribution, the $\ln \left[ - \ln \left( S \left( t \right) \right) \right]$ is a linear func-
tion of $\ln \left( t \right)$ with slope $p$ and intercept $p \ln \left( \lambda \right) .$
If the slope equals one then $t$ follows an expo-
nential distribution.

We again return to the remission data and
evaluate the appropriateness of the Weibull
assumption for the treated $\mathrm { \left( T R T \right. } = 1 \mathrm { \left. \right) }$ and pla-
cebo $\left( \mathrm { T R T } = 0 \right.$ groups. On the left is the plot
of the $\log$ negative $\log$ Kaplan-Meier survival
estimates against the $\log$ of time for $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$
and $\mathrm { T R T } = 0 .$ Both plots look reasonably
straight suggesting that the Weibull assump-
tion is reasonable. Furthermore, the lines
appear to have the same slope (i.e., are parallel,
same $\left. p \right)$ suggesting that the PH (and thus the
AFT) assumptions hold. If this common slope
equals one (i.e., $p =$ 1), then survival time fol-
lows an exponential distribution. The Weibull
model output containing the parameter esti-
mates includes a statistical test for the hypo-
thesis $p = 1$ or equivalently for $\ln \left( p \right) = 0$ (for
testing the exponential assumption). This is
examined later in this section.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="306 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

Summary of possible results
for plot of In[-In $\left. \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right]$ against $\ln \left( t \right)$

1\. Parallel straight lines => Weibull,
PH, and AFT assumptions hold

2\. Parallel straight lines with slope
of $\mathrm { t 1 } \Rightarrow$ Exponential. PH and AFT

3\. Parallel but not straight lines =>
PH but not Weibull, not AFT
(can use Cox model)

4\. Not parallel and not straight =>
Not Weibull, PH violated

5\. Not parallel but straight $\mathrm { l i n e s } \Rightarrow$
Weibull holds, but PH and AFT
violated, different p

Previous plot suggests Weibull and
PH assumption reasonable for TRT

Weibull PH model:

$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \lambda p t ^ { p - 1 }$
where $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) .$

Hazard ratio $\left( \mathrm { T R T } = 1 \right.$ vs. $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$

$$\mathrm { H R } = \frac { \mathrm { e x p } \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right) p t ^ { p - 1 } } { \mathrm { e x p } \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) p t ^ { p - 1 } }$$
$$= \exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \right)$$

On the left is a summary of five possible
results from an examination of the $\log$ negative
log Kaplan-Meier survival estimates plotted
against the $\log$ of time for two or more levels
of covariates. The key points are that straight
lines support the Weibull assumption and
parallel curves support the PH assumption.
If the plots are parallel but not straight then the
PH assumption holds but not the Weibull.
Assessing whether the curves are parallel is a
familiar approach for evaluating the PH
assumption in a Cox model (see Chapter 4
and Computer Appendix). An interesting sce-
nario occurs if the lines are straight but not
parallel. In this situation the Weibull assump-
tion is supported but the PH and AFT assump-
tions are violated. If the lines are not parallel,
then $p$ is not constant across levels of covari-
ates. In Section IX of this chapter, we present a
method for modeling the shape parameter $p$ as
a function of predictor variables, but typically
$p$ is assumed fixed.

An examination of the plot on the previous
page suggests that the Weibull and PH assump-
tions are reasonable for treatment (TRT). First
the PH form of the model is presented and then
the AFT form.

The Weibull hazard function is $h \left( t \right) = \lambda p t ^ { p - 1 } .$
A Weibull PH model is defined by reparameter-
izing lambda $\lambda$ as $\exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right.$ The hazard
ratio is obtained by substituting $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ and
$\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ into the hazard functions (see left).
After canceling we obtain the familiar result
$\exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \right) .$ Note that this result depends on $p$ hav-
ing the same value for $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ and $\mathrm { T R T } = 0 ,$
otherwise time (t) would not cancel in the
expression for the HR (i.e., PH assumption
not satisfied).

$$\widehat { H R } \left( \mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s . } 0 \right) = \exp \left( - 1 . 7 3 1 \right)$$

$= 0 . 1 8$

Weibull: $\widehat { H R } = 0 . 1 8$
Exponential: $\widehat { H R } = 0 . 2 2$
Suggests preventive effect of TRT

Comparing Cox and Weibull PH
models

Cox: estimate $\beta _ { 1 }$
$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 } \left( t \right) \exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$
1

baseline hazard unspecified

Weibull: estimate $\beta _ { 0 } ,$ $\beta _ { 1 } , p$
$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \lambda p t ^ { p - 1 }$ where
$\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) .$
$h \left( t \right) = \left[ \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) p t ^ { p - 1 } \right] \exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } T R T \right) .$
7

baseline hazard specified paramet-
rically

On the left is stata output from running the PH
form of the Weibull model. There are para-
meter estimates for the coefficient of TRT, the
intercept (called _cons), and for three forms of
the shape parameter: $p ,$ $1 / p ,$ and $\log \left( p \right) .$ The
estimate for $p$ is 1.366 suggesting an increase
in the hazard as survival time increases (because
$\widehat { p } > 1 .$ A statistical test for $H _ { 0 } : \log \left( p \right) = 0$ yields
a p-value of 0.034. At a significance level of 0.05
we would reject the null and decide $p$ is not
equal to 1, suggesting that the exponential
model is not appropriate.

An estimated hazard ratio of 0.18 is obtained
by exponentiating the estimated coefficient
$\left( - 1 . 7 3 1 \right)$ of the TRT variable. The 95% confi-
dence interval for this HR is calculated to be
$\left( 0 . 0 8 , 0 . 4 0 \right)$ indicating a significant preventive
effect of treatment. These results are similar to
those obtained from the exponential model in
which the estimated hazard ratio was 0.22.

It can be instructive to compare the Cox and
Weibull PH models. The Cox PH model with
treatment as the only predictor is stated as $\mathrm { h } _ { 0 } \left( t \right)$
$\exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) .$ There is one parameter to esti-
mate $\left( \beta _ { 1 } \right)$ and the distribution of the baseline
hazard $\left( \mathrm { h } _ { 0 } \left( t \right) \right)$ remains unspecified.

With some manipulation, the Weibull PH
model can also be expressed as a product of a
baseline hazard and $\exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right.$ (see left).
There are three parameters to estimate $\beta _ { 0 } ,$ $\beta _ { 1 } ,$
and $p$ that fully specify the hazard.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="308 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

$$S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t ^ { p } \right)$$

solve for $\mathrm { t }$

$$t = \left[ - \ln S \left( t \right) \right] ^ { 1 / p } \times \frac { 1 } { \lambda ^ { 1 / p } }$$

$\mathrm { l e t } \frac { 1 } { \lambda ^ { 1 / p } } = \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { R X } \right)$
$t = \left[ - \ln S \left( t \right) \right] ^ { 1 / p } \times \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T \right.$

$$7$$

Let $S \left( t \right) = q$

$t = \left[ - \ln \left( q \right) \right] ^ { 1 / p } \times \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T \right)$
Median survival time $\left( q = 0 . 5 \right)$

$$t _ { m } = \left[ - \ln \left( 0 . 5 \right) \right] ^ { 1 / p }$$
$$\times \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$$

Acceleration factor, $\gamma \left( \mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s } \right.$
$\mathrm { T R T } = 0$

$$\gamma = \frac { \left[ - \ln \left( q \right) \right] ^ { 1 / p } \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \right) } { \left[ - \ln \left( q \right) \right] ^ { 1 / p } \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } \right) }$$
$$= \exp \left( \alpha _ { 1 } \right)$$

An AFT model can also be formulated with the
Weibull distribution. We develop the AFT
parameterization similarly to that done with
the exponential model, by solving for t in
terms of a fixed $S \left( t \right) .$ The Weibull survival func-
tion is $S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t ^ { p } \right) .$ Taking the natural $\log ,$
multiplying by negative 1, raising to the power
$1 / p ,$ and then multiplying by the reciprocal of
$\lambda ^ { 1 / p } ,$ yields the expression for t shown on the
left. By reparameterizing $1 / \lambda ^ { 1 / p } = \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \right.$
$\alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } ,$ it can be shown that $\alpha _ { 1 } ,$ the coefficient
of the predictor variable TRT, is used to scale
the time to any fixed value of $\mathrm { S } \left( t \right)$ (see left).

The expression for $t$ is restated on the left in
terms of any fixed probability $S \left( t \right) = q .$ For
example, to find an expression for the median
survival time $t _ { \mathrm { m } } ,$ substitute $\mathrm { q } = 0 . 5$ (see left).

The acceleration factor $\gamma$ is obtained as the
ratio of the times to $S \left( t \right) = q$ for $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ and
for $\mathrm { T R T } = 0 .$ After canceling, $\gamma$ reduces to exp
$\left( \alpha _ { 1 } \right) ,$ where $\alpha _ { 1 }$ is the coefficient of the TRT
variable. As with the PH form of the model,
this result depends on $p$ not varying by treat-
ment status; otherwise $\gamma$ would depend on $\mathrm { q } .$

Output from running a Weibull AFT model is
shown on the left. The estimates for each form
of the shape parameter $\left( p , 1 / p , \right.$ and $\ln \left( p \right)$ are
the same as obtained from the previously
shown PH form of the model.

The estimated acceleration factor of 3.55 is
obtained by exponentiating the estimated
coefficient (1.267) of the TRT variable. The
95% confidence interval for $\gamma$ is calculated to
be (1.93, 6.53). There results are shown at the
top of the following page.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: VII. Log-Logistic Example 309" -->

Weibull AFT:

$$\widehat { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { T R T } = 1 \mathrm { v s . } 0 \right) = \mathrm { e x p } \left( 1 . 2 6 7 \right)$$
$$= 3 . 5 5$$

$9 5 \% \text { CI } = \text { exp } \left[ 1 . 2 6 7 \pm 1 . 9 6 \left( 0 . 3 1 1 \right) \right]$
$= \left( 1 . 9 3 , 6 . 5 3 \right)$

Weibull: $\widehat { \gamma } = 3 . 5 5$
Exponential: $\widehat { \gamma } = 4 . 6 0$ (assumes
$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \lambda$

Relating Weibull AFT and
PH coefficients

$\mathrm { A F T } : \lambda ^ { 1 / p } = \exp \left[ - \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) \right]$
$\left( 1 / p \right) \ln \lambda = - \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T \right)$
$\ln \lambda = - p \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$

PH: $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$
In $\lambda = \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T }$

$$R e l a t i o n s h i p \quad o f \quad c o e f f i c i e n t s :$$

$\beta _ { j } = - \alpha _ { j } p$
$$\begin{array}{} { \beta _ { j } = - \alpha _ { j } p \text { so that } } \\ { \beta = - \alpha \text { for exponential } \left( p = 1 \right) } \end{array}$$
$\beta = - \alpha$ $\left( p = 1 \right.$

Relating estimates for TRT
(PH vs. AFT)

$- 1 . 7 3 1 = \left( - 1 . 2 6 7 \right) \left( 1 . 3 6 6 \right)$

Next: log-logistic model

· Hazard may be nonmonotonic

Weibull model

· Hazard does not change
direction

These results suggest that the median (or any
other quantile of) survival time is increased by
a factor of 3.55 for those receiving the treat-
ment compared to the placebo. Recall that the
acceleration factor was estimated at 4.60 using
the exponential model. However, the exponen-
tial model uses a much stronger assumption:
that the hazards are constant.

Corresponding coefficients obtained from the
PH and AFT forms of the Weibull models are
related as follows: $\beta _ { j } = - \alpha _ { j } p$ for the jth covari-
ate. This can most easily be seen by formulat-
ing the parameterization equivalently in terms
of $\ln \left( \lambda \right)$ for both the PH and $\mathrm { A F T }$ forms of the
model as shown on the left.

This relationship is illustrated utilizing the
coefficient estimates we obtained for TRT:
$- 1 . 7 3 1 = \left( - 1 . 2 6 7 \right) \left( 1 . 3 6 6 \right) .$ Note for the expo-
nential model in which $p = 1 ,$ the $P H$ and AFT
coefficients are related, $\beta = - \alpha .$

In the next example the log-logistic model is
presented. In contrast to the Weibull, the haz-
ard function for the log-logistic distribution
allows for some nonmonotonic behavior in
the hazard function.


## VII. Log-Logistic Example

Log-logistic hazard: $h \left( t \right) = \frac { i p t ^ { p - 1 } } { 1 + i t ^ { p } }$
p
$\left. p > 0 \text { and } \lambda > 0 \right)$

The log-logistic distribution accommodates an
AFT model but not a PH model. Its hazard
function is shown on the left. The shape
parameter is $p \left( > 0 \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="310 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->


### Shape of hazard function:

$p \leq 1$ hazard decreases over time
$p > 1$ hazard first increases and
then decreases over time
(unimodal)

Log-logistic modeling assumptions:


<figure>

AFT
PH

AFT
PO

</figure>


PO: Survival Odds ratio constant over time

Survival odds

$$\frac { S \left( t \right) } { \left( 1 - S \left( t \right) \right) } = \frac { P \left( T > t \right) } { P \left( T \leq t \right) }$$

Failure odds by time t

$$\frac { \left( 1 - S \left( t \right) \right) } { S \left( t \right) } = \frac { P \left( T \leq t \right) } { P \left( T > t \right) }$$

Log-logistic survival and failure
functions

$$S \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + 2 t ^ { p } } 1 - S \left( t \right) = \frac { 2 t ^ { p } } { 1 + \lambda t ^ { p } }$$


### Failure odds

$$\frac { 1 - S \left( t \right) } { S \left( t \right) } = \frac { \left( \frac { 2 t ^ { p } } { 1 + \lambda t ^ { p } } \right) } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 + \lambda t ^ { p } } \right) } = 2 t ^ { p }$$

Log-logistic PO model:

· Reparameterize $\lambda$ in terms of
$\mathrm { X s }$ and $\beta s$
$\mathrm { S O R } = \frac { \mathrm { S } _ { 1 } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) / \left( 1 - \mathrm { S } _ { 1 } \left( t \right) \right) } { \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \left( t \right) / \left( 1 - \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \left( t \right) \right) }$

· SOR satisfies PO if SOR
constant over time

· SOR constant

m

$\mathrm { F O R } = 1 / \mathrm { S O R }$ constant

If $p \leq 1$ then the hazard decreases over time.
If $p > 1 ,$ however, the hazard increases to a
maximum point and then decreases over time.
In this case $\left( p > 1 \right) ,$ the hazard function is said
to be unimodal.

Unlike the Weibull model, a log-logistic AFT
model is not a PH model. However, the log-
logistic AFT model is a proportional odds
(PO) model. A proportional odds survival
model is a model in which the survival
odds ratio is assumed to remain constant
over time. This is analogous to a proportional
hazard model where the hazard ratio is
assumed constant over time.

The survival odds is the odds of surviving beyond
time $\mathrm { t }$ (i.e., $S \left( t \right) / \left( 1 \quad - \quad S \left( t \right) \right) .$ This is the probability
of not getting the event by time t divided by the
probability of getting the event by time t.

The failure odds is the odds of getting the
event by time $t$ (i.e., $\left. \left( 1 - \mathrm { S } \left( t \right) \right) / \mathrm { S } \left( t \right) \right) ,$ which is
the reciprocal of the survival odds (see left).

The log-logistic survival function $\left( S \left( t \right) \right)$ and
failure function $\left( 1 - \mathrm { S } \left( t \right) \right)$ are shown on the left.

The failure odds simplifies in a log-logistic
model to $\lambda t ^ { p }$ (see left).

A log-logistic proportional odds model can be
formulated by reparameterizing $\widehat { \lambda }$ in terms of
predictor variables and regression parameters.
We come back to this point later in this section.

A survival odds ratio (SOR) is defined as the
ratio of survival odds for two groups of sub-
jects, as shown on the left.

An SOR satisfies a PO assumption provided the
SOR does not depend on time.

Also, if the SOR does not depend on time, then
the failure odds ratio $\left( \mathrm { F O R } = 1 / \mathrm { S O R } \right)$ also does
not depend on time.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: VII. Log-Logistic Example 311" -->

Log Odds Is Linear with $\ln \left( t \right)$

$\log \left( \text { failure odds } \right) = \ln \left( \lambda t ^ { p } \right)$

$$\begin{array}{} { = \ln \left( \lambda \right) + p \left[ \ln \left( t \right) \right] } \\ { 7 } \\ { \text { Intercept } = \ln \left( \lambda \right) \quad \text { slope } = p } \end{array}$$

Evaluate log-logistic assumption
graphically

· Plot
$\ln \left[ \frac { \left( 1 - \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right) } { \widehat { S } \left( t \right) } \right.$
against $\ln \left( t \right)$

· If log-logistic, then plot is linear
with $\mathrm { s l o p e } = p$


### Alternatively

· Plot
$\ln \left( \frac { \left( \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right) } { \left( 1 - \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right) } \right)$
against $\ln \left( t \right)$

· If log-logistic, then plot is linear
with $\mathrm { s l o p e } = - p$


#### Remission Data

WBCCAT: white blood cell count
variable $\mathrm { m e d i u m } = 1$ vs. $\mathrm { h i g h } = 2$

</figure>


The log of the failure odds is $\ln \left( \lambda t ^ { p } \right) ,$ which can
be rewritten as $\ln \left( \lambda \right) + p \left[ \ln \left( t \right) \right] .$ In other words,
the log odds of failure is a linear function of
the log of time with slope $p$ and intercept $\ln \left( 2 \right) .$
This is a useful result enabling a graphical
evaluation for the appropriateness of the log-
logistic distribution.

The log-logistic assumption can be graphically
evaluated by plotting $\ln \left( 1 - \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right) / \left( \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right)$ against
$\ln \left( t \right)$ where $\widehat { S } \left( t \right)$ are the Kaplan-Meier sur-
vival estimates. If survival time follows a $\log$
logistic distribution, then the resulting plots
should be a straight line of $s l o p e \quad p .$

We could alternatively plot the log of the
survival odds, $\ln \left( \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right) / \left( 1 - \widehat { S } \left( t \right) \right) ,$ against $\ln \left( t \right) .$
If the log-logistic assumption is correct
the resulting plots should be a straight line of
$\mathrm { s l o p e } - p .$

We next consider a different variable from the
remission data: a dichotomous variable for
white blood cell count (WBCCAT) coded
medium = 1 and $\mathrm { h i g h } = 2 .$

On the left is the plot of the $\log$ odds of
survival (obtained from the Kaplan-Meier sur-
vival estimates) against the $\log$ of time com-
paring medium $\left( \mathrm { W B C C A T } = 1 \right)$ and high
$\left( \mathrm { W B C C A T } = 2 \right.$ blood cell counts. The points
for WBCCAT = 1 lie above the points for
$\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 2$ indicating that the survival odds
are higher for those with a medium white blood
cell count compared to high. The lines look rea-
sonably straight and parallel, at least until the
estimated odds of survival approaches zero.

If we accept the proposition that the lines look
straight, then the log-logistic assumption is
reasonable. Because the lines look parallel,
the proportional odds (PO) assumption is also
reasonable. If the PO assumption holds in a
log-logistic model then the AFT assumption
also holds.

## Acceleration factor, $\gamma \left( \mathrm { W B C C A T } = 2 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { W B C C A T } = 1 \right)$

$$= \frac { \left[ q ^ { - 1 } - 1 \right] ^ { 1 / p } \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \right) } { \left[ q ^ { - 1 } - 1 \right] ^ { 1 / p } \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + 1 \alpha _ { 1 } \right) }$$
$$= \exp \left( \alpha _ { 1 } \right)$$

$p = 1 / \left( 0 . 4 5 9 \right) = 2 . 1 8$

$\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 2$ vs. $\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 1$
(log-logistic):

$\widehat { \gamma } = \exp \left( - 0 . 8 7 1 \right) = 0 . 4 2$

Failure odds

$$\frac { 1 - S \left( t \right) } { S \left( t \right) } = \frac { \left( \frac { 2 t ^ { p } } { 1 + 2 t ^ { p } } \right) } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 + 2 t ^ { p } } \right) } = 2 t ^ { p }$$

where $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right.$ WBCCAT)

$O R \left( W B C C A T = 2 \quad v s . \right.$ WBCCAT = 1)

$$= \frac { t ^ { p } \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + 2 \beta _ { 1 } \right) } { t ^ { p } \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + 1 \beta _ { 1 } \right) } = \exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \right)$$

The acceleration factor $\gamma$ is found by taking the
ratio of the times to $S \left( t \right) = q$ for $\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 2$
and for $\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 1$ 1. After canceling, $\gamma$
reduces to $\exp \left( \alpha _ { 1 } \right) ,$ where $\alpha _ { 1 }$ is the coefficient
of the WBCCAT variable in the $A F T$ model.

The output from running the AFT log-logistic
model is shown on the left. The coefficient esti-
mate for WBCCAT is -0.871, which is statisti-
cally significant with a p-value of 0.003 (far
right column of output).

Stata provides estimates of the reciprocal of
$p \left( \mathrm { g a m m a } = 1 / p \right)$ rather than for p. The esti-
mate for gamma is 0.459. Therefore, the esti-
mate $\mathrm { f o r } p$ is $1 / \left( 0 . 4 5 9 \right) = 2 . 1 8 .$

An estimate of the acceleration factor $\widehat { \gamma }$ com-
paring $\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 2$ to $\mathrm { W B C C A T } = 1$ is found
by exponentiating the estimate -0.871 of $\alpha _ { 1 }$ to
obtain 0.42. The 95% confidence interval for $\gamma$
is calculated to be (0.23, 0.75).

These results suggest that the time for going
out of remission is "accelerated" for patients
with a high white blood cell count compared
to those with a medium count by an estimated
factor of 0.42. In terms of the survival functions
estimated from this model, $\widehat { S } _ { 1 } \left( t \right) = \widehat { S } _ { 2 } \left( 0 . 4 2 t \right)$
where $\widehat { S } _ { 1 } \left( t \right)$ and $\widehat { S } _ { 2 } \left( t \right)$ are the respective survival
functions for patients with medium and high
blood cell counts.

The proportional odds form of the log-logistic
model can also be formulated by reparameter-
izing $\widehat { } .$ Recall that the log-logistic failure odds
is $\lambda t ^ { p } .$

By setting $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right.$ WBCCAT), a (failure)
odds ratio comparing WBCCAT = 2 to WBCCAT
$= 1$ can be calculated (see left). After canceling,
the odds ratio reduces to $\exp \left( \beta _ { 1 } \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="314 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

Comparing AFT and PO
(log-logistic)
Relationship of coefficients:

$$\beta _ { \mathrm { i } } = - \alpha _ { \mathrm { j } } p$$

Since $\widehat { \alpha } = - 0 . 8 7 1$ and $\widehat { p } = 2 . 1 8$
Then,

$$\widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } = - \left( - 0 . 8 7 1 \right) \left( 2 . 1 8 \right) = 1 . 9 0$$

and

$$\widehat { O R } = \exp \left( 1 . 9 0 \right) = 6 . 6 9$$

The corresponding coefficients for log-logistic
PO and AFT models are related by $\beta _ { \mathrm { j } } = - \alpha _ { \mathrm { j } }$
$p$ for the jth covariate. This result is obtained
using a similar argument to that presented for
the Weibull example in the previous section.

The estimate for $\alpha _ { 1 }$ in the AFT model is -0.871
and the estimate for $p$ is 2.18. Therefore, an
estimate for $\beta _ { 1 }$ can be found by multiplying
$- \left( - 0 . 8 7 1 \right)$ times 2.18 yielding 1.90. An estimate
of the odds ratio is found by exponentiating
this estimate, $\exp \left( 1 . 9 0 \right) = 6 . 6 9 .$ (Unfortunately,
neither Stata nor SAS estimates the propor-
tional odds form of the model.)


## VIII. A More General Form of the AFT Model

Exponential: $S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t \right)$

· AFT Form:
$\frac { 1 } { 2 }$
$= \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \check { \alpha } _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$

· PH Form: $\widehat { \lambda }$
$= \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$

Weibull: $S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t ^ { p } \right)$

1

· AFT Form:
$\frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 1 / p } }$
$= \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$

· PH Form: $\widehat { x }$

$= \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$

Log-logistic: $S \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + 2 t ^ { p } }$
1

1

· AFT Form:
$\frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 1 / p } }$
$= \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { W B C C A T } \right)$

· PO Form: $\widehat { \lambda }$
$= \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { W B C C A T } \right)$

On the left is a summary of the models
discussed in the previous sections. These
models were formulated by reparameterizing
the survival (and hazard) functions in terms of
regression parameters and predictor variables.

An advantage for stating the models in this
form is that the interpretation and relation-
ships between parameters are specific to each
distribution.

However, there are more general ways these
models could be stated. The Cox PH model is
a more general way of stating the proportional
hazards model. In this section we discuss a
more general formulation of the AFT model.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: VIII. A More General Form of the AFT Model 315" -->

General Form of AFT Model
(One Predictor)

$\ln \left( T \right) = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T + \epsilon$
1
random error


<figure>
</figure>


With additional parameter

$$\ln \left( T \right) = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T + \sigma \epsilon$$

V
$\sigma$ scales the error

If $\epsilon \sim \mathrm { N } \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ then

$$\ln \left( T \right) \sim N \left( \mu = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T , s d = \sigma \right)$$

Similar to linear regression (except
for inclusion of censorships)

In general,

$\mu _ { \ln \left( \mathrm { T } \right) } \neq \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) , \mathrm { s d } \neq \mathrm { o }$

Interpretation of parameters de-
pends on distribution

Let $\sigma = \frac { 1 } { p }$ , then
p

$\ln \left( \mathrm { T } \right) = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } + \frac { 1 } { p } \in$

Additive model in terms of $\ln \left( T \right)$
but
multiplicative model in terms of $\mathrm { T }$

$$\mathrm { T } = \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } + \frac { 1 } { p } \epsilon \right)$$
$$= \exp \left[ \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) \right] \times \exp \left( \frac { 1 } { p } \epsilon \right)$$

Consider an AFT model with one predictor
(TRT) in which $\mathrm { T }$ represents a random variable
for survival time. The model can be expressed
on the log scale as shown on the left, where $\epsilon$ is
random error following some distribution.

Some distributions have an additional para-
meter (6) scaling $6$ . The model including this
additional parameter can be expressed as
shown on the left, where the random error $\epsilon$ is
multiplied by a scale parameter o.

If $\epsilon$ follows a standard normal distribution and
$\ln \left( T \right) = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T + \sigma \epsilon ,$ then $\ln \left( T \right)$ would
follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu =$
$\alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T }$ and standard deviation o. For this
situation, the model would look like a standard
linear regression. The key difference between
fitting this survival model and a standard linear
regression is the inclusion of censored obser-
vations in the data.

In general, for other then the normal distribu-
tion, the mean of $\ln \left( \mathrm { T } \right)$ is not necessarily $\alpha _ { 0 } +$
$\alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T }$ and its standard deviation is not o. In
other words, it should not be assumed that the
mean of $\epsilon$ is 0 and the standard deviation is 1.
The interpretation of the parameters depends
on the underlying distribution.

Sometimes the model is parameterized using
$\sigma = 1 / p .$ The model can then be restated by
replacing $\sigma \epsilon$ with $\left( 1 / p \right)$ €.


## The AFT model is additive on the $\log$ scale but $a$ multiplicative model with respect to $\mathrm { T } .$

In particular, the model can be expressed in
terms of $\mathrm { T }$ by exponentiating $\ln \left( \mathrm { T } \right) ,$ as shown
on the left.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## 316 7. Parametric Survival Models


<table>
<tr>
<td>Collapse $\alpha _ { 0 }$ into baseline term $\mathrm { T } _ { 0 } = \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } \right) \exp \left( \frac { 1 } { p } \epsilon \right)$</td>
<td>The model may also be expressed by collapsing the intercept into a baseline random term $\mathrm { T } _ { 0 }$ (see left). In this setting, $\mathrm { T } _ { 0 }$ is a random vari- able representing the survival time of the pla- cebo group $\mathrm { \left( T R T \right. } = 0 \mathrm { \left. \right) } .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>so that $\mathrm { T } = \mathrm { T } _ { 0 }$ exp(a1 TRT) where $\mathrm { T } _ { 0 }$ is a random variable for $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$</td>
<td>Thus, the AFT model has a form analogous to the a Cox PH model, although the baseline term $\mathrm { T } _ { 0 }$ is a random variable rather than a constant.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>AFT model may be expressed in terms of $\mathrm { T }$ or $\ln \left( T \right)$</td>
<td rowspan="2">In summary, an AFT model may be expressed by reparameterizing a specific distribution, or may be more generally expressed either in terms of a random variable $\mathrm { T }$ (for survival time), or In (T). If $\mathrm { T }$ follows a Weibull distribution then In (T) follows a distribution called the extreme</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Comparing Distributions: $T$ and $\ln \left( \mathrm { T } \right)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>T ln(T)</td>
<td>minimum value distribution (see table on left).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Exponential Extreme minimum value</td>
<td>Similarly, if T follows a log-logistic or lognormal distribution then $\ln \left( T \right)$ follows a logistic or</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Weibull Extreme minimum value</td>
<td rowspan="2">normal distribution, respectively. The logistic and normal distributions are similarly shaped, and are both symmetric about their mean.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Log-logistic Logistic Lognormal Normal</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>IX. Other Parametric Models</td>
<td>In the previous sections we presented examples of the exponential, Weibull, and log-logistic models. In this section we briefly discuss some other parametric survival models.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Generalized Gamma Model</td>
<td rowspan="3">The generalized gamma model is a parametric survival model that is supported by both SAS and Stata software. The hazard and survival function for this model is complicated and can only be expressed in terms of integrals. The generalized gamma distribution has three para- meters allowing for great flexibility in its shape. The Weibull and lognormal distributions are special cases of the generalized gamma distri- bution (see Practice Exercises 12 to 14).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· Supported by SAS and Stata · $S \left( t \right) ,$ $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right)$ expressed in terms of integrals</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· Contains three parameters · Weibull, lognormal are special cases</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Lognormal Model</td>
<td rowspan="2">The lognormal model also has a relatively complicated hazard and survival function that can only be expressed in terms of integrals. The shape of the lognormal distribution is very sim- ilar to the log-logistic distribution and yields similar model results. A difference is that although the lognormal model accommodates an accelerated failure time model, it is not a proportional odds model.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Similar to log-logistic Difference: Log-logistic: AFT and PO Lognormal: AFT but not PO</td>
</tr>
</table>


<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: IX. Other Parametric Models 317" -->


### Gompertz Model

· PH model but not AFT

· One predictor (TRT) in model:

$\mathrm { h } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) = \left[ \exp \left( \gamma \mathrm { t } \right) \right] \times \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$
☒
1
parametrically specified
$\mathrm { h } _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) = \exp \left( \gamma \mathrm { t } \right)$

$\gamma > 0$ hazard exponentially

☒
increases with $t$
$\gamma < 0$ hazard exponentially
☒
decreases with t
$\gamma = 0$ constant hazard
(exponential model)

AFT model: multiplicative

$$\mathrm { T } = \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } + \epsilon \right)$$

$= \exp \left( \alpha _ { 0 } \right) \times \exp \left( \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right) \times \exp \left( \epsilon \right)$

but

additive on log scale:
$\ln \left( T \right) = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } T R T + \epsilon$

Additive failure time model
$\mathrm { T } = \alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } + \epsilon$
☒
1

$\mathrm { T }$ rather than $\log \left( \mathrm { T } \right)$ is linear with
TRT

Modeling the Shape Parameter
(e.g., Weibull and log-logistic)

Typical Weibull model

$$h \left( t \right) = 2 p t ^ { p - 1 }$$

where $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$
$p$ unaffected by predictors

Parametric models need not be AFT models.
The Gompertz model is a parametric propor-
tional hazards model but not an AFT model.
The model can be expressed in a form similar
to that of a Cox PH model except that the
baseline hazard is specified as the hazard of
a Gompertz distribution containing a shape
parameter $\gamma$ (see left).

If $\gamma > 0$ then the hazard exponentially increases
over time. If $\gamma < 0$ then the hazard exponen-
tially decreases over time. If $\gamma = 0$ then the
hazard is constant and reduces to the exponen-
tial model.

The AFT model is a multiplicative model (i.e.,
a multiplicative scaling of failure time). It
becomes an additive model on the log scale
(see left side).

An alternative parametric model is to define an
additive failure time model in terms of $\mathrm { T } ,$
rather than $\ln \left( T \right) .$ Consider the model: $\mathrm { T } =$
$\alpha _ { 0 } + \alpha _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } + \epsilon .$ Now T, rather than $\ln \left( T \right) ,$ is
expressed as a linear function of the regression
parameters. SAS supports such an additive fail-
ure time model (see Computer Appendix).

Many parametric models contain an extra
shape (or ancillary) parameter beyond the
regression parameters. For example, the Wei-
bull and log-logistic models contain a shape
parameter p. Typically, this parameter is con-
sidered fixed, unaffected by changes in the
values of predictor variables.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="318 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

Alternative Weibull model
models the ancillary parameter $p$
$h \left( t \right) = \lambda p t ^ { p - 1 }$

where $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$
$p = \exp \left( \delta _ { 0 } + \delta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T R T } \right)$

Not a PH or AFT model if $\delta _ { 1 } \neq 0$
but still a Weibull model


### Choosing appropriate model

· Evaluate graphically
o Exponential

o Weibull

o Log-logistic

· Akaike's information criterion

o Compares model fit

o Uses -2 log likelihood

An alternative approach is to model the shape
parameter in terms of predictor variables and
regression coefficients. In the Weibull model
shown on the left, both $\widehat { x }$ and $p$ are modeled
as functions of treatment status (TRT). If $\delta _ { 1 }$ is
not equal to zero, then the value of $p$ differs by
TRT. For that situation, the PH (and thus the
AFT) assumption is violated because $t ^ { p - 1 }$ will
not cancel in the hazard ratio for TRT (see
Practice Exercises 15 to 17).

Choosing the most appropriate parametric
model can be difficult. We have provided
graphical approaches for evaluating the appro-
priateness of the exponential, Weibull, and log-
logistic models. Akaike's information crite-
rion (AIC) provides an approach for compar-
ing the fit of models with different underlying
distributions, making use of $t h e \quad - 2$ log likeli-
hood statistic (described in Practice Exercises
11 and 14).

X. The Parametric
Likelihood

· Function of observed data and
unknown parameters

· Based on outcome $\mathrm { d i s t r i b u t i o n }$
f(t)

· Censoring complicates survival
data

o Right-censored

o Left-censored

o Interval-censored

The likelihood for any parametric model is
a function of the observed data and the
model's unknown parameters. The form of the
likelihood is based on the probability density
function f(t) of the outcome variable. A compli-
cation of survival data is the possible inclusion
of censored observations (i.e., observations
in which the exact time of the outcome is
unobserved). We consider three types of
censored observations: right-censored, left-
censored, and interval-censored.


### Examples of Censored Subjects


<figure>

☒
time
Right-censored:

1
10
X

Left-censored:
X
☒

1
10

time

Interval-censored:
1
8
X
☒
time

1
10

</figure>


Right-censored. Suppose a subject is lost to
follow-up after 10 years of observation. The
time of event is not observed because it hap-
pened after the 10th year. This subject is right-
censored at 10 years because the event happened
to the right of 10 on the time line (i.e., $\left. t > 1 0 \right) .$

Left-censored. Suppose a subject had an event
before the 10th year but the exact time of the
event is unknown. This subject is left-censored
at 10 years (i.e., $\left. t < 1 0 \right) .$

Interval-censored. Suppose a subject had an
event between the 8th and 10th year (exact
time unknown). This subject is interval-
censored (i.e., $\left. 8 < t < 1 0 \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: $X .$ The Parametric Likelihood 319" -->

Formulating the Likelihood

Barry, Gary, Larry, ... , Outcome
Distribution $f \left( t \right)$


<table>
<tr>
<th>Subject</th>
<th>Event Time</th>
<th>Likelihood Contribution</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Barry</td>
<td>$t = 2$</td>
<td>$f \left( 2 \right)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Gary</td>
<td>$t > 8$ (right-censored)</td>
<td>1 $\int _ { 8 } ^ { \infty } f \left( t \right) d t$ 8</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Harry</td>
<td>$t = 6$</td>
<td>$f \left( 6 \right)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Carrie</td>
<td>$t < 2$ (left-censored)</td>
<td>2 $\int _ { 0 } f \left( t \right) d t$ 0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Larry</td>
<td>$4 < t < 8$ (interval-censored)</td>
<td>8 $\int f \left( t \right) d t$ 4</td>
</tr>
</table>


### Likelihood $\left( L \right)$

Product of individual contributions

$$L = f \left( 2 \right) x \int _ { 8 } ^ { \infty } f \left( t \right) d t \times f \left( 6 \right)$$
$$\times \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } f \left( t \right) d t \times \int _ { 0 } ^ { 8 } f \left( t \right) d t$$
$$\mathrm { \left( B a r r y \right. } \times \mathrm { G a r y } \times \mathrm { H a r r y }$$
$$\left. \times \mathrm { C a r r i e } \times \mathrm { L a r r y } \right)$$


#### Assumptions for formulating L

· No competing risks

o Competing event does not
prohibit event of interest

o Death of all causes is classic
example of no competing
risk

· Subjects independent

o Allows L to be formulated as
product of subjects'
contributions

The table on the left illustrates how the likeli-
hood is formulated for data on five subjects.
We assume a probability density function $\mathrm { f } \left( t \right)$
for the outcome. Barry gets the event at time
$t = 2 .$ His contribution to the likelihood is $f \left( 2 \right) .$
Gary is right-censored at $t =$ 8. The probability
that Gary gets the event after $t = 8$ is found by
integrating $\mathrm { f } \left( t \right)$ from 8 to infinity. This is Gary's
contribution to the likelihood. Harry gets the
event at time $t = 6 .$ His contribution to the
likelihood is f(6). Carrie is left-censored at $t =$
2. Her contribution to the likelihood is
obtained by integrating f(t) from zero to 2.
Finally, Larry is interval-censored between $t =$
4 and $t = 8 .$ His contribution to the likelihood is
found by integrating f(t) from 4 to 8.

The full likelihood (L) is found by taking the
product of each subject's independent contri-
bution to the likelihood. The likelihood for this
example is shown on the left.

The formulation of this likelihood uses the
assumption of no competing risks. In other
words, we assume that no competing event
will prohibit any subject from eventually get-
ting the event of interest (see Chapter 9). Death
from all causes is the classic example of an
outcome that in reality has no competing risk.
For other outcomes, the no competing risk
assumption is more of a theoretical construct.

Another assumption is that individual contri-
butions to the likelihood are independent. This
assumption allows the full likelihood to be for-
mulated as the product of each individual's
contribution.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="320 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

· Follow-up time continuous
o No gaps in follow-up

Revisit example with Barry, Gary,
Larry, ...
$\mathrm { f } \left( t \right)$ is Weibull
SMOKE is only predictor

$$1 = \mathrm { S m o k e r }$$

$$0 = \mathrm { N o n s m o k e r }$$

Weibull: $h \left( t \right) = h p t ^ { p - 1 } ,$
$S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - 2 t ^ { p } \right)$

$f \left( t \right) = h \left( t \right) S \left( t \right)$
$f \left( t \right) = 2 p t \quad p - 1 \quad e x p \left( - A t p \right)$

where $\lambda = \exp \left( \beta 0 + \beta 1 \mathrm { S M O K E } \right)$
(PH form of the model)

Data Layout for Right-, Left-, and
Interval-Censoring Using SAS

<tr>
<th>ID</th>
<th>LOWER</th>
<th>UPPER</th>
<th>SMOKE</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Barry</td>
<td>2</td>
<td>2</td>
<td>1</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Gary</td>
<td>8</td>
<td>-</td>
<td>0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Harry</td>
<td>6</td>
<td>6</td>
<td>0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Carrie</td>
<td>-</td>
<td>2</td>
<td>0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Larry</td>
<td>4</td>
<td>8</td>
<td>1</td>

</table>


Right-censored: UPPER missing
Left-censored: LOWER missing
Interval-censored: LOWER <
UPPER

Not censored: $\mathrm { L O W E R } = \mathrm { U P P E R }$

A third assumption is that each subjects
follow-up time is continuous without gaps (i.e.,
once subjects are out of the study, they do not
return). If gaps are allowed, the likelihood can be
modified to accommodate such a scenario.

In the last example, we did not specify the
probability density f(t), nor did we specify any
covariates. We revisit this example, assuming
$f \left( t \right)$ is Weibull with one predictor SMOKE in
the model (coded 1 for smokers and 0 for
nonsmokers).

The Weibull hazard and survival functions are
shown on the left. The probability density func-
tion f(t) is the product of the hazard and sur-
vival functions. The parameterization will use
the proportional hazards (PH) form of the
Weibull model: $\lambda = \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 }$ SMOKE.

On the left is the data layout for running para-
metric models containing right-, left-, and
interval censored data in a form suitable for
using the SAS procedure PROC LIFETEST
(version 8.2). There are two time variables
LOWERand UPPER. Barry got the event at
$t = 2 ,$ so both LOWER and UPPER get the
value 2. Gary was right-censored at 8 $\left( t < 8 \right)$
so LOWER gets the value 8 and UPPER is set to
missing. Carrie is left-censored at 2 $\left( t > 2 \right)$ so
LOWER is set to missing and UPPER gets the
value 2. Larry was interval-censored with
$L O W E R = 4$ and $\mathrm { U P P E R } = 8 .$ Barry and Larry
are smokers whereas Gary, Harry, and Carrie
are nonsmokers.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: XI. Interval-Censored Data 321" -->


### Weibull Likelihood (L)

Product of individual contributions

$$L = f \left( 2 \right) \times \int _ { 8 } ^ { \infty } f \left( t \right) d t \times f \left( 6 \right) \times \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } f \left( t \right) d t$$
$$\times \int _ { 4 } ^ { 8 } f \left( t \right) d t$$
$$L = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right) p \left( 2 \right) ^ { p - 1 } \exp \left( - \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right) 2 ^ { p } \right)$$
$$\times \int _ { 8 } ^ { \infty } \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) p \left( t \right) ^ { p - 1 } \exp \left( - \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) t ^ { p } \right) d t$$
$$\times \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) p \left( 6 \right) ^ { p - 1 } \exp \left( - \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) 6 ^ { p } \right)$$
$$\times \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) p \left( t \right) ^ { p - 1 } \exp \left( - \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) t ^ { p } \right) d t$$
$$\times \int _ { 4 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right) p \left( t \right) ^ { p - 1 } } { \times \exp \left( - \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \right) t ^ { p } \right) d t }$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

The full likelihood using the Weibull distribu-
tion can now be formulated as a product of
each individual's contribution (shown on the
left). We have used a small dataset (five sub-
jects) for ease of illustration but the process
can be generalized for any number of subjects.

Obtaining maximum likelihood
estimates

Solve system of equations:

$\frac { \partial L n \left( L \right) } { \partial \beta _ { j } } = 0 \quad \mathrm { j } = 1 , 2 , \ldots , \mathrm { N }$

where $\mathrm { N } = \#$ of parameters

Once the likelihood is formulated, the question
becomes: which values $o f$ the regression para-
meters would maximize L? The process of max-
imizing the likelihood is typically carried out
by setting the partial derivative of the natural
$\log \mathrm { o f } L$ to zero and then solving the system of
equations (called the score equations). The
parameter estimates $\left( \mathrm { e } . \mathrm { g } . , \widehat { p } , \widehat { \beta } _ { 0 } , \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } \right)$ that maxi-
mize L are the maximum likelihood estimates.


## XI. Interval-Censored Data

Parametric likelihood

· Handles right-, left-, or interval-
censored data


### Cox likelihood

· Designed to handle right-
censored data.

One advantage of a parametric model com-
pared to a Cox model is that the parametric
likelihood easily accommodates right-, left-, or
interval-censored data. The Cox likelihood, by
contrast, easily handles right-censored data
but does not directly accommodate left- or
interval-censored data.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="322 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

Interval-censored study design

· Check for nonsymptomatic
outcome once a year

· If outcome newly detected,
exact time occurred during
previous year

· Left-censoring special case of
interval-censoring

o Zero the lower boundary of
the interval

Parametric model can be fitted

· $f \left( t \right)$ specified

· Contribution to likelihood for
each subject

o Integrate $\mathrm { f } \left( t \right)$ over event
interval


### Binary regression

· Alternative approach for
interval-censored data

· Outcome coded

o $0 \quad i f \quad s u b j e c t \quad s u r v i v e s \quad i n t e r v a l$

o 1 if subject gets event during
interval

· Useful approach if

o Ample number of events in
each interval

o Prefer not to specify $f \left( t \right)$

Information on Three Subjects

Subject 1: Gets event in first
interval

Subject 2: Survives first interval
Survives second
interval Gets event in
third interval

Subject 3: Survives first interval
Gets event in second
interval

Sometimes the design of a study is such that all
the data are interval-censored. For example,
consider a study in which healthcare workers
examine subjects once a year, checking for a
nonsymptomatic outcome. If an event was first
detected in the beginning of the third year, then
the exact time of the outcome occurred some-
time between the second and third years. In
this framework left-censoring can be consid-
ered a special case of interval-censoring with
zero as the lower boundary of the interval.

A parametric model can easily be fitted using
the methods described in the previous section.
Once a distribution for the outcome, $f \left( t \right) ,$ is
specified, each subject's contribution to the
likelihood is obtained by integrating f(t) over
the interval in which he or she had the event.

A binary regression (e.g., logistic regression)
is an alternative approach that may be consid-
ered if all the data are interval-censored. With
this method, the outcome variable can be
coded zero if the subject survives the interval
and coded one if the subject gets the event
during the interval. This approach is particu-
larly useful if there are an ample number of
events in each interval and the analyst prefers
not to specify a distribution $f \left( t \right)$ for continuous
survival time.

For illustration, consider a small dataset con-
taining three subjects. Subject 1 gets the event
in the first interval of follow-up, subject 2 gets
the event in the third interval, and subject 3 gets
the event in the second interval of follow-up.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: $X I .$ Interval-Censored Data 323" -->

EVENT: dichotomous outcome
coded 1 if event, 0 for no event dur-
ing the interval

$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } :$ dummy variables for
intervals 1, 2, and 3 coded 1 if in the
corresponding interval, 0 otherwise

TRT: Treatment coded 1 for new
treatment, 0 for placebo

Logistic Model

Logit $\mathrm { P } \left( \mathrm { Y } = 1 \right) = \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$

$$+ \beta _ { 3 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } + \beta _ { 4 } \mathrm { T R T }$$

where $\mathrm { P } \left( \mathrm { Y } = 1 \right)$ is the probability
of event for a given interval
conditioned on survival of previous
intervals

Interpretation of Parameters

B1: Log odds of event in 1st
interval among $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$
$\beta _ { 2 } :$ Log odds of event in 2nd
interval given survival of 1st
interval among $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$
$\beta _ { 3 } :$ Log odds of event in 3rd
interval given survival of first
two intervals among $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$

$\beta _ { 4 } :$ Log odds ratio for TRT

The data layout is shown on the left. Each obser-
vation represents one interval of follow-up time
allowing multiple observations per subject.
EVENT is the dichotomous outcome variable.
Subject 1 had the event in the first interval
$\mathrm { \left( E V E N T \right. } = 1 \mathrm { \left. \right) }$ and thus has one observation.
Subject 2 has three observations because she
survived the first two intervals (EVENT = 0)
but got the event in the third interval. $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ is a
dummy variable coded 1 if the observation
represents the first interval and 0 otherwise.
Similarly, $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ is coded 1 for the second interval
and $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 }$ is coded 1 for the third interval.

TRT is the predictor of interest, coded 1 for
the new treatment and 0 for the placebo.
TRT could be coded as a time-independent
or time-dependent variable. In this example,
TRT is time-independent because TRT does
not change values over different intervals
corresponding to the same subject.

A logistic model (shown at left) containing the
three dummy variables and TRT can be formu-
lated with the data in this form.

Care must be taken with the interpretation of
the parameters: $\beta _ { 1 }$ is the $\log$ odds of the event
occurring in the first interval among the pla-
cebo group; $\beta _ { 2 }$ is the $\log$ odds of the event
occurring in the second interval conditioned
on survival of the first interval among the pla-
cebo group; $\beta _ { 3 }$ is the $\log$ odds of the event
occurring in the third interval conditioned on
survival of the first and second intervals among
the placebo group; and $\beta _ { 4 }$ is the $\log$ odds ratio
for TRT.

<!-- PageBreak -->


#### 324 7. Parametric Survival Models


<table>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 }$ play similar role as inter- cept</td>
<td>The dummy variables play a similar role to that of the intercept in a conventional regression, providing a baseline outcome measure for</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· Baseline measure when covariates are zero</td>
<td>the case in which all predictors are zero (e.g., $\mathrm { T R T } = 0 .$ In general, the baseline measure may</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· 3 parameters rather than 1 intercept</td>
<td rowspan="2">differ for each interval, which is the reason that the model contains 3 dummy variables rather than 1 intercept.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>o Baseline measure may differ for each interval</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Odds Ratio $\mathrm { \left( T R T \right. } = 1 \mathrm { v s } .$ $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ $= \exp \left( \beta _ { 4 } \right)$</td>
<td>The odds ratio comparing $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ to $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ is obtained by exponentiating B4. This model uses the proportional odds (PO) assumption</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Model uses PO assumption</td>
<td>in that the odds ratio is assumed constant over time (or at least constant at the end of each</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· OR constant over time · PO assumption can be tested o Include interaction terms with TRT and dummy variables</td>
<td>interval). This assumption can be tested by including interaction (product) terms with TRT and two of the dummy variables in the model. A statistically significant product term would suggest a violation of the PO assump-</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>o Significant interaction suggests PO violation</td>
<td rowspan="2">tion. However, if there are sparse data corresponding to particular intervals, it will not be practical to carry out such a test on those intervals.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>o Need ample data to practically carry out test</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Alternative Binary Model</td>
<td rowspan="2">Logistic regression is not the only type of binary regression that may be considered for interval-censored data. An alternative binary model (shown on the left) uses the comple- mentary log-log link function rather than</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$$\log \left( - \log \left( 1 - \mathrm { P } \left( \mathrm { Y } = 1 \right) \right) \right)$$ $$= \beta _ { 1 } D _ { 1 } + \beta _ { 2 } D _ { 2 } + \beta _ { 3 } D _ { 3 } + \beta _ { 4 } T R T$$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>where $1 - \mathrm { P } \left( \mathrm { Y } = 1 \right)$ is the probabil- ity of surviving a given interval conditioned on survival of previous intervals</td>
<td>the logit link function that is used for the more familiar logistic regression.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Complementary log-log link</td>
<td>A model using a complementary log-log link</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· Log-log survival modeled as linear function of regression parameters</td>
<td>function expresses the log negative log survival probability as a linear function of regression parameters. By contrast, a model using a logit link function expresses the log odds of failure</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Logit link</td>
<td rowspan="2">(i.e., getting the event) as a linear function of regression parameters.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>· Log odds of failure modeled as linear function of regression parameters</td>
</tr>
</table>


<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: $X I .$ Interval-Censored Data 325" -->


##### Complementary log-log model is PH model

· HR (TRT = 1 vs. $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$
$= \exp \left( \beta _ { 4 } \right)$

· HR constant over time

Log-log survival curves:
parallel => additive effects
$\Rightarrow \mathrm { P H }$

Complementary log-log link:
additive effects on log-log scale
=>PH

In theory

· Survival time is continuous

In practice

· Survival time measured in
intervals

o If event occurred in month 7
then event occurred in an
interval of time

Discrete survival analysis

· Discrete time

· for example, number of
menstrual cycles to pregnancy
rather than time to pregnancy

o Fraction of cycle does not
make sense

The complementary log-log binary model is
a proportional hazards model. The hazard
ratio comparing $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ to $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ is
obtained by exponentiating $\beta _ { 4 } .$

Recall we can use log-log survival curves to
evaluate the PH assumption for a Cox model.
If the effects are additive (e.g., parallel for TRT
$= 1$ and $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ then the PH assumption is
assumed to hold. The underlying idea is similar
for the complementary log-log link function in
that additive effects are assumed on the log-log
scale (e.g., comparing $\mathrm { T R T } = 1$ to $\mathrm { T R T } = 0$ 0).

In theory, the time-to-event variable in survival
analyses is thought of as a continuous variable.
In practice, however, the time variable is typi-
cally an interval of time. For example, if time is
measured in months and an event occurs in
month 7 then the event is recorded as having
occurred in a specific interval lasting a month.

Discrete survival analysis is a survival analy-
sis in which the outcome variable is discrete,
both in theory and in practice. For example,
consider a study in which women who stop
using oral contraception are followed until
pregnancy. The outcome is defined as the num-
ber of menstrual cycles until pregnancy. The
number of cycles rather than the time to preg-
nancy is used because the cycle length varies
among women and a woman ovulates only
once per menstrual cycle (i.e., one opportunity
per cycle to become pregnant). The number of
cycles is a discrete outcome. $A$ fraction of a
cycle does not make sense.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="326 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

Analyzing discrete survival data

· Can use binary regression

· Analogous to interval-censored
data

o Discrete outcome -
subjects survive discrete
units of time

o Interval outcomes -
subjects survive intervals of
time

Binary regression, as described in this section,
can be applied for discrete survival outcomes
in a similar manner to that $d e s c r i b e d$ for
interval-censored outcomes. With this method,
subjects can be conceptualized as surviving
discrete units of time analogously as subjects
surviving continuous intervals of time.


## XII. Frailty Models


### Frailty

· Random component

· Accounts for extra variability
from unobserved factors

Conceptualize S(t) two ways:

· For an individual

· Averaging over a theoretical
large population

With Frailty Component

Jake and Blake

1\. May have different S(t) due to
unobserved factors

2\. Extra source of variability in
outcome (e.g., more variation
than expected under Weibull)

Without Frailty Component

Jake and Blake

1\. Have same $S \left( t \right)$

2\. May have different event times
because event time is random,
following some distribution
(e.g., Weibull)

In this section we consider the inclusion of
frailty to a survival model. Frailty is a random
component designed to account for variability
due to unobserved individual-level factors that
is otherwise unaccounted for by the other pre-
dictors in the model.

Consider a survival model with a continuous
age variable and dichotomous smoking status
variable as the only predictors. Under this
model the survival function for a 33-year-old
smoker might be conceptualized in different
ways. One way is as the survival function for
an individual 33-year-old smoker. The second
way is as some kind of averaging over a theoret-
ical large population of 33-year-old smokers.

Now suppose a "frailty" component is included
in the model. Under this model, we can concep-
tualize survival functions specific to each indi-
vidual. If Jake and Blake are both 33-year-old
smokers, not only might their observed failure
times be different, but under this model their
individual survival functions could also be
different. Jake may be more "frail" than Blake
due to unobserved factors accounting for indi-
vidual level differences in his hazard and sur-
vival functions. These unobserved factors may
contribute an extra layer of heterogeneity,
leading to greater variability in survival times
than might be expected under the model (e.g.,
Weibull) without the frailty component.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: XII. Frailty Models 327" -->


### The frailty component $\alpha \left( \alpha > 0 \right)$

· Unobserved multiplicative
effect on hazard

· Follows distribution $g \left( \alpha \right)$ with
$\mu = \mathrm { E } \left( \alpha \right) = 1$

· $\mathrm { V a r } \left( \alpha \right) = \theta ,$ parameter to be
estimated

The frailty $\alpha$ is an unobserved multiplicative
effect on the hazard function assumed to fol-
low some distribution $g \left( \alpha \right)$ with $\alpha > 0$ and the
mean of $\alpha$ equal to 1. The variance of $\alpha$ is a
parameter 0 (theta) that is typically estimated
from the data.

Hazard and survival conditioned
on frailty
$h \left( t | \alpha \right) = \alpha h \left( t \right)$
$S \left( t | \alpha \right) = S \left( t \right) ^ { \alpha }$

$\alpha > 1$

· Increased hazard: $\alpha h \left( t \right) > h \left( t \right)$

· Decreased survival: $S \left( t \right) ^ { \alpha } < S \left( t \right)$

$\alpha < 1$

· Decreased hazard: $\alpha h \left( t \right) < h \left( t \right)$

· Increases survival: $S \left( t \right) ^ { \alpha } > S \left( t \right)$

$\alpha = 1$ (average frailty): $\alpha h \left( t \right) = h \left( t \right)$


### Survival functions (with frailty models)

1\. Conditional, $\mathrm { S } \left( t | \alpha \right) ,$ individual
level

2\. Unconditional, $S _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( t \right) ,$
population level

Unconditional survival function
$S _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( t \right)$

$$S _ { U } \left( t \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } S \left( t | \alpha \right) g \left\{ \alpha \right\} d \alpha$$
$$h _ { U } \left( t \right) = \frac { - d \left[ S _ { U } \left( t \right) \right] / d t } { S _ { U } \left( t \right) }$$

An individual's hazard function conditional on
the frailty can be expressed as a multiplied by
$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) .$ Using the relationship between the sur-
vival and hazard functions, the corresponding
conditional survival function can be expressed
as S(t) raised to the x power.

Individuals with $\alpha > 1$ have an increased haz-
ard and decreased probability of survival com-
pared to those of average frailty $\left( \alpha = 1 \right.$ 1).
Similarly, individuals with $\alpha < 1$ have a
decreased hazard and increased probability of
survival compared to those of average frailty.

With frailty models, we distinguish the individ-
ual level or conditional survival function
$S \left( t | \alpha \right)$ discussed above, from the population
level or unconditional survival function
$S _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( t \right) ,$ which represents a population average.
Once the frailty distribution $g \left( \alpha \right)$ is chosen, the
unconditional survival function is found by
integrating over the conditional survival func-
tion $S \left( t | \alpha \right)$ times $g \left( \alpha \right) ,$ with respect to a. The
corresponding unconditional hazard $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$ can
then be found using the relationship between
the survival and hazard functions (see left).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="328 7. Parametric Survival Models" -->

Frailty distribution $g \left( \alpha \right) ,$ a > 0, Any distribution for $\alpha > 0$ with a mean of 1
$\mathrm { E } \left( \alpha \right) = 1$
can theoretically be used for the distribution
of the frailty. Stata supports two distributions:
Stata offers choices for $g \left( \alpha \right)$
the gamma distribution and the inverse-
1\. Gamma
Gaussian distribution for the frailty. With
2\. Inverse-Gaussian
the mean fixed at 1, both these distributions
Both distributions parameterized
in terms of 0, where $\mathrm { V a r } \left( \alpha \right) = \theta$
are parameterized in terms of the variance 0
and typically yield similar results.

To illustrate the use of a frailty model, we apply
the data from the Veteran's Administration
Lung Cancer Trial described in Chapter 5. The
exposure of interest is treatment status TX
(standard $= 1 ,$ $t e s t =$ 2). The control variables
are performance status (PERF), disease dura-
tion (DD), AGE, and prior therapy (PRIORTX),
whose coding is shown on the left. The out-
come is time to death (in days).

Output from running a Weibull PH model
without frailty using Stata software is shown
on the left (Model 1). The model can be
expressed: $h \left( t \right) = h p t ^ { p - 1 }$ where

$$\lambda = \exp \left( \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { T X } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { P E R F } + \beta _ { 3 } \mathrm { D D } \right.$$
$$\left. + \beta _ { 4 } A G E + \beta _ { 5 } P R I O R T X \right) .$$

The estimate of the hazard ratio comparing
$\mathrm { T X } = 2 \mathrm { v s } .$ $\mathrm { T X } = 1$ is $\exp \left( 0 . 1 3 7 \right) = 1 . 1 5$
controlling for performance status, disease
duration, age, and prior therapy. The estimate
for the shape parameter is 0.982 suggesting a
slightly decreasing hazard over time.
