Because the CIC curve describes "cumulative
incidence", a plot of the curve starts at 0 when
$t = 0$ and is a nondecreasing function up until
the latest time of individual follow-up $\left( t = 2 4 . 4 \right) .$
We show on the left the graphs of both the
$\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$ and $1 - \mathrm { K M } _ { \mathrm { c a } } .$ Notice that $1 - \mathrm { K M } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$
overestimates the probability of failure for the
event-type "death from cancer (ca)."

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="450 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->


### CIC Summary

· Gives marginal probability.

· Does not use product limit
formulation

· Not included in mainstream
commercially avilable
statistical packages (e.g., SAS,
STATA, SPSS)

Thus, as the example illustrates, the "marginal
probability" estimated by the CIC does not use
a product-limit (i.e., KM) formulation. More-
over, the computation of a CIC is currently not
included in mainstream commercially available
statistical packages.

Independence of competing risks
not required for CIC approach.

Nevertheless, CIC requires

$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c 1 } } \left( t \right) + \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c 2 } } \left( t \right) + \ldots + \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c k } } \left( t \right)$
where

$$\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) = \text { overall hazard }$$
$$\mathrm { h } _ { c } \left( t \right) = \text { hazard for event type } c$$

Note: Satisfied if

· Mutually exclusive event types

. Nonrecurrent events

Comparing CIC's for two or more
groups:

· Statistical test available (Gray,
1989)

· Analogous to log-rank test

· No independence assumption

· Does not adjust for covariates


<figure>

0.5

Cumulative incidence: Death due to cancer

Proportion dying

0.4

0.2 0.3

placebo or 0.2mg DES

1.0mg or 5.0mg DES

0.1

0.0

0

12

24

36

48

60

72

Months from randomisation

</figure>


As mentioned earlier, the assumption of inde-
pendent competing risks is not required for the
calculation of the CIC, in contrast to the KM
survival curve, which requires this assumption.

Nevertheless, the CIC does require that the
overall hazard is the sum of the individual
hazards for all the risk types (Kalbfleisch and
Prentice, 1980). The latter assumption will
be satisfied, however, whenever competing
risks are mutually exclusive and events are
nonrecurrent, i.e., one and only one event can
occur at any one time and only once over time.

Gray (1988) developed a test to compare two or
more CICs. This test is analogous to the log-
rank test. The independence assumption is not
required. However, this test does not adjust for
covariates.

The plot shown on the left gives the CICs for the
two treatments for the Byar data that we origi-
nally introduced in Section III of this chapter.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: VI. Cumulative Incidence Curves (CIC) 451" -->

Gray's test results: $x ^ { 2 } = 6 . 6 ,$ $\mathrm { d f } = 1$
P-value: 0.01

PH model used to obtain CIC
$\Downarrow$
Independence of competing risks
required
(but CIC meaningful for treatment
utility)

Modeling CIC with covariates using
PH model: Fine and Gray (1999)


## (CIC also called subdistribution function)

Software available (Accord, 1997)

Fine and Gray model analogous to
Cox PH model:

· use a hazard function defined
$f r o m \quad a \quad C I C$

· effects of predictors (e.g., HRs)
have similar interpretation


## Sub-distribution hazard function (for event-type c):

$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right) = \lim _ { \Delta \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \mathrm { P r } \left( t < \mathrm { T } _ { c } < t + \Delta t | \mathrm { B } \right) } { \Delta t }$


<figure>

Where $\mathrm { B } = \left\{ \mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { c } } > t \right.$ tor $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { c } ^ { \prime } } \leq t ,$ $c ^ { \prime } \neq c$

either
$\mathrm { s u r v i v a l } > t$ from event-type c
☒
or

$\leq t$ from a different event-type $\left( c ^ { \prime } \right)$

</figure>


Using Gray's test to compare the two CICs
shown in the plot, we find the two curves to
be significantly different $\left( \mathrm { P } = 0 . 0 1 \right) .$

So far, we have described the CIC without
considering (Cox PH) models that account for
covariates. However, when a PH model is
used to obtain hazard ratio estimates for indi-
vidual competing risks as an intermediate step
in the computation of a CIC, the indepen-
dence of competing risks is required. In any
case, the CIC has a meaningful interpretation
in terms of treatment utility regardless of
whether competing risks are independent.

Fine and Gray (1999) provide methodology for
modeling the CIC with covariates using a pro-
portional hazards assumption. They refer to
the CIC curves as subdistribution functions.
Software is available that allows for such
models to be fitted (Accord, 1997).

The CIC models developed by Fine and Gray
are analogous to the Cox PH model but, for any
failure type, they model a hazard function
(also called a sub-distribution hazard) derived
from a CIC. The results from fitting these
models have a similar interpretation regarding
the effects of predictors in the model as can be
derived from the (standard) Cox PH model
approach for competing risks data.

The (sub-distribution) hazard function for
event-type c used in the Fine and Gray method
is defined by $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right)$ on the left. This function,
based on the CIC, gives the hazard rate for
a cause-specific event at time t based on the
risk set that remains at time t after accounting
for all previously occurring event-types (i.e.,
including competing risks).

In the above hazard formula, the expression
defined by $\mathrm { B }$ to the right of the conditioning
symbol $\left( | \right)$ in the formula conditions on a sub-
ject's surviving from event-type c past time t or
on a subject's failing from a different event-type
$\left( e . g . , c ^ { \prime } \right.$ at or before time $t ;$ this accounts for the
occurrence of all event-types prior to time t.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="452 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

Equivalent formula for $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right) :$

$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right) = \frac { \mathrm { d C I C } _ { c } \left( t \right) / \mathrm { d } t } { 1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { c } \left( t \right) }$
1

$$a n a l o g o u s \quad t o$$

Hazard when no competing risks:

$$h \left( t \right) = \frac { f \left( t \right) } { S \left( t \right) }$$

$$w h e r e \quad f \left( t \right) = p r o b a b i l i t y \quad d e n s i t y \quad f u n c t i o n$$
$$= \lim _ { \Delta t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \Pr \left[ t \leq T < t + \Delta t \right] } { \Delta t }$$

The analogy:


<table>
<tr>
<th>Risks</th>
<th>No Competing Risks</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\frac { \text { Competi } } { \text { ha,crc } \left( t \right) }$</td>
<td>$h \left( t \right)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { d C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( t \right) / d t$</td>
<td>$\mathrm { f } \left( t \right) = d \mathrm { F } \left( t \right) / d t$ where $\mathrm { F } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) = \mathrm { P r } \left( \mathrm { T } \leq t \right)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( t \right)$</td>
<td>$\mathrm { S } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) = 1 - \mathrm { F } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$</td>
</tr>
</table>

Note: 1 - CICc(t) is not strictly a survival
curve since the formula for $\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( t \right)$ uses
overall survival S(t) in its calculation
rather than survival from event-type $C$ c,
i.e., $\mathrm { S } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$


## Fine and Gray's CIC model:

$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( \exp \right) \left[ \sum _ { \mathrm { i } = 1 } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \right]$$ ☒

PH assumption satisfied in above
model:

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( \mathrm { X } ^ { * } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \exp \left[ \sum _ { \mathrm { i } = 1 } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \gamma _ { \mathrm { i } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { * } - \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \right) \right.$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

$a n a l o g o u s \quad t o$

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C o x P H } } \left( \mathrm { X } ^ { * } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \exp \left[ \sum _ { \mathrm { i } = 1 } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \beta _ { \mathrm { i } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { * } - \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \right) \right.$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

Can use "extended" Fine and
Gray model to account for time-
dependent variables.

An equivalent mathematical expression for
$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right)$ is shown on the left, where $\mathrm { d C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( t \right) / d t$
is the derivative of the CIC function for event-
type $c$ at time $t .$

This expression for $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right)$ is analogous to a
similar expression shown on the left for the
hazard function $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right)$ when there are no com-
peting risks (see Chapter 7, Section II). In the
latter case, $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right)$ is equal to the probability
density function f(t) divided by the survival
function S(t) shown on the left.

To clarify the analogy further, the term in
$\mathrm { d C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) / \mathrm { d } t$ in the numerator of the formula
for $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right)$ corresponds to the density function
$f \left( t \right)$ in the numerator of $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) .$ Note that $\mathrm { f } \left( t \right)$
is equal to $d F \left( t \right) / d t ,$ where $\mathrm { F } \left( t \right)$ is cumulative
distribution function $\mathrm { F } \left( t \right)$ in other words, the
derivative of $\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( t \right)$ in $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right)$ corresponds to
the derivative of $\mathrm { F } \left( t \right) ,$ which is $\mathrm { f } \left( t \right)$ in the expres-
sion for $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) .$

Also, when there are no competing risks, $S \left( t \right)$
equals $1 - \mathrm { F } \left( \mathrm { t } \right) ,$ which is analogous to the
denominator $1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$ of $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right) .$ Strictly
speaking, however, $1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { c } \left( t \right)$ is not directly
equivalent to a survival curve for event-type c,
since the formula CICc(t) treats the occurrence
of events from other event-types as failures
rather than as censored observations.

The CIC model developed by Fine and Gray is
shown on the left. This model is analogous to
a Cox model, except that the model considers
the subdistribution hazard function $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { C I C } } \left( t \right)$
instead of the hazard function $\mathrm { h } \left( t \right) .$

The model shown here satisfies the PH assump-
tion for the subpopulation hazard being mod-
eled, i.e., the general HR formula is essentially
the same as for the Cox model, except that the
B's in the Cox PH model are now replaced by $\gamma ^ { \prime } s$
in the Fine and Gray model.

The Fine and Gray model can be extended to
allow for variables not satisfying the PH,
including time-dependent variables.

For example, the estimated coefficient of Rx is
-0.414 in Table 9.10 vs. - 0.550 in Table 9.1.
The corresponding hazard ratio estimates $\left( e ^ { \beta } \right)$
are 0.661 $\left( = 1 / 1 . 5 1 3 \right)$ and 0.577 $\left( = 1 / 1 . 7 3 3 \right) ,$
respectively, so that the strength of the associ-
ation is slightly weaker using the Fine and Gray
approach for these data, although both hazard
ratios are highly significant.


## VII. Conditional Probability Curves (CPC)

A third measure of failure risk: CPC
(Other measures: 1-KM and CIC)

$\mathrm { C P C } _ { c } = \mathrm { P r } \left( \mathrm { T } _ { c } \leq t \mid \mathrm { T } \geq t \right)$

where $\mathrm { T } _ { c } = \mathrm { t i m e } \mathrm { u n t i l } \mathrm { e v e n t } c \mathrm { o c c u l }$

$$\mathrm { T } = \text { time until any compet }$$
$$i n g \quad r i s k \quad e v e n t \quad o c c u r s$$

Another approach to competing risks is called
the cumulative conditional probability or
CPC. CPCs provide a third summary measure,
in addition to (1 - KM) and CIC, of the risk of
failure of an event in the presence of competing
risks. Put simply, the $\mathrm { C P C } _ { c } .$ is the probability of
experiencing an event c by time t, given that an
individual has not experienced any of the other
competing risks by time t.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="454 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

$\mathrm { C P C } _ { c } = \mathrm { C I C } _ { c } / \left( 1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { c ^ { \prime } } \right)$

where $\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c } ^ { \prime } } = \mathrm { C I C }$ from risks other
than c

Graphs of CPC's obtained from CIC's
Tests to compare CPC's:
Pepe and Mori (1993) -2 curves
Lunn (1998) -g curves


### Example of CPC calculation

· $\mathrm { n } = 2 4$ subjects

· all subjects receive treatment
XRT for head and neck cancer

· $\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$ previously calculated
(Table 9.9)

For event-type c, the CPC is defined by $\mathrm { C I C } _ { c }$
divided by $\left( 1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { c ^ { \prime } } \right) ,$ where $\mathrm { C I C } _ { c }$ is the cumu-
lative incidence of failure from risks other than
risk c (i.e., all other risks considered together).

Graphs of CPC curves can be obtained from
CIC curves and have been studied by Pepe-
Mori (1993) and Lunn (1998). Pepe-Mori pro-
vide a test to compare two CPC curves. Lunn
(1998) extended this test to $\mathrm { g }$ g-groups and allow
for strata.

We illustrate the calculation of a CPC using the
previously described hypothetical study data
involving 24 individuals receiving radiotherapy
(XRT) for the treatment of head and neck can-
cer (ca). For these data, the calculations for the
$\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$ were given in previous Table 9.9, below
which was shown the graph of the $\mathrm { C I C } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$ curve.

Table 9.11 on the left gives the calculation of
the CPC for the event-type "death from can-
$C e r ^ { \prime \prime } .$ A graph of the $\mathrm { C P C } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$ curve is shown
below the table.

From the $\mathrm { C P C } _ { \mathrm { c a } }$ curve, we can see, for example,
that at 10 months, the probability of dying from
cancer given that no other type of event has
occurred is 0.455. Similarly, at 20.3 months,
the probability of dying of cancer given that no
other type of event has occurred is 0.860.

Returning to the Byar dataset originally
introduced in Section III of this chapter, the
plot shown here gives the CPC curves compar-
ing the two DES treatments. These curves give
the probability of an event (death) from pros-
tate cancer at any particular time given that the
patient is alive at this time to experience the
event.

(Note: the Fine and Gray approach has not
been extended to model the CPCs in a regres-
sion framework.)

The Pepe-Mori test shows a significant differ-
ence between these two CPC curves.


## VIII. Method 2-The Lunn-McNeil (LM) Approach

Method I: separate estimates for
each failure type, treating the com-
peting failure types as censored


### Method 2: LM Approach

· Uses a single Cox (PH) model

· Gives identical results as
obtained from Method 1

· Allows flexibility to perform
statistical inferences not
available from Method 1

We have previously (Section IV) described an
approach (called Method 1) for analyzing com-
peting risks data that uses the Cox (PH) model
to separatelyestimate hazards and correspon-
ding hazard ratios for each failure type,
treating the other(competing) failure types as
censored in addition?to those not failing from
any event-type.

We now describe Method 2, called the Lunn-
McNeil (LM) approach, that allows only one
Cox PH model to be fit rather than separate
models for each event-type (i,e., Method I
above). This approach, depending on the vari-
ables put in the model, can give identical
results to those obtained from separate models.
Moreover, the LM approach allows flexibility
to perform statistical inferences about various
features of the competing risk models that can
not be conveniently assessed using Method 1.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="456 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

To carry out the LM approach, the data layout
must be augmented. If there are C competing
risks, the original data must be duplicated
$\mathrm { C }$ times, one row for each failure type as
shown in Table 9.12 for the ith subject with
survival time $t i$ in the table. Also, C dummy
variables $D _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } , \ldots ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ are created as
shown in the table. The value of the status
variable $\mathrm { e } _ { \mathrm { c } } ,$ with c going from 1 to $\mathrm { C } ,$ equals 1
if event type c occurs at time c, and equals 0 if
otherwise. The Xs in the table denote the pre-
dictors of interest and, as shown in the table,
are identical in each row of the table.

The dummy variables $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ are
indicators that distinguish the C competing
risks (i.e., event-types).

Thus, the dummy variable $\mathrm { D } _ { c }$ equals 1 for
event-type c and 0 otherwise.

For example, for event type 1, the Ds are $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } = 1 ,$
$\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } = 0 ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } = 0 , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } = 0$ for event-type 2, the
Ds are $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } = 0 ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } = 1 ,$ $D _ { 3 } = 0 , \ldots , D _ { C } = 0$ and
for event-type 3, the Ds are $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } = 0 ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } = 0 ,$ $D _ { 3 } =$
$1 , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } = 0 .$

Table 9.13 shows observations for subject #s 1,
14, 16, and 503 from the Byar dataset. The $\mathrm { C A } ,$
CVD, and OTH columns denote the $\mathrm { C } = 3$
dummy variables $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } ,$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } ,$ respectively.
The last three columns, labeled $\mathrm { R x } ,$ Age, and
Wt give values for three of the eight predictors.

In this table, there are three lines of data for each
subject, which correspond to the three compet-
ing risks, Cancer death, CVD death, and Other
death, respectively. The survival time (Stime)
for subject 1 is 72, for subject 14 is 49, for subject
16 is 3, and for subject 503 is 41.

<td>To use the LM approach with augmented data to obtain identical results from fitting separate models (Method 1), an interaction version of a</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>stratified Cox PH model is required. A general form for this model based on the notation used for the column heading variables in Table 9.12 is shown at the left.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td colspan="4">$+ \delta _ { 3 1 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 3 2 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { 3 p } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$ $$+ \cdots .$$ $$\left. + \delta _ { \mathrm { C 1 } } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { C 2 } } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { \mathrm { C p } } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$$ 1st row: predictors $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ 2nd row: product terms $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$</td>
<td rowspan="2">Recall that the $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { p }$ denote the p pre- dictors of interest. $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } , \ldots ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ are $C \quad - \quad 1$ dummy variables that distinguish the $\mathrm { C }$ event- types. Note that event-type 1 $\left( g = 1 \right)$ is the referent group, so variable $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ is omitted from the model. Thus, the first row in the exponen- tial formula contains the Xs, the second row contains product terms involving $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ with each of the Xs, and so on, with the last (Cth) row containing product terms of $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ with each of the $\mathrm { X } s .$ The strata $\left( \mathrm { g } = 1 , \ldots , \mathrm { C } \right)$ are the $\mathrm { C }$ event- types.</td>
</tr>

<td colspan="5">$$\mathrm { h } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 1 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$$ $$\times \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \beta _ { \mathrm { p } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$$ $$\left( \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } = \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } = \cdots = \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } = 0 \right)$$</td>

<!-- PageHeader="458 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

No product terms in model:

$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { g } = 1 } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 0 \right) = \mathrm { e x p } \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \right]$
☒

Product terms $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ in model:
☒

$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { g } = 1 } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 0 \right)$
☒ ☒
$= \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } + \Sigma \beta _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \right]$

LM Hazard Model for
Event-Type $\mathrm { T v p e } g \left( > 1 \right)$

$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { \theta g } } ^ { * } \left( t \right)$$
$$\times \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \beta _ { \mathrm { p } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right.$$
$$\left. + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 2 } } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { \mathrm { g p } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$$
$$= \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { 0 g } } ^ { * } \left( t \right) \exp \left[ \left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right) \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \left( \beta _ { 2 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 2 } } \right) \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } \right.$$
$$\left. + \cdots + \left( \beta _ { \mathrm { p } } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g p } } \right) \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

No product terms $X _ { 1 } X _ { 1 }$ in the
☒
model and $\mathrm { g } > 1 :$

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { g } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 0 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left[ \left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right) \right]$$ ☒

Product terms $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ in the model
☒
and $\mathrm { g } > 1 :$

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { g } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 0 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left[ \left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right) \right.$$
$$\left. + \Sigma \left( \beta _ { \mathrm { j } } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g j } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \right) \right]$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

Thus, for $\mathrm { g } = 1 ,$ if $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ is a $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$ variable, the
other Xs are covariates, and there are no prod-
uct terms $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ in the model, the formula for
☒

☒
the HR for the effect of $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ adjusted for the
covariates is $\exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \right] .$ The more general expo-
nential formula described in Chapter 3 would
need to be used instead to obtain adjusted HRs
if there are interaction terms in the model of
the form $X _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } .$
☒

For any $\mathrm { g }$ greater than 1, the general hazard
model simplifies to a hazard function formula
that contains only those product terms involv-
ing the subscript $\mathrm { g } ,$ because $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { g } } = 1$ and $D _ { g ^ { \prime } } =$
0 for $\mathrm { g } ^ { \prime }$ not equal to $\mathfrak{g} _ { 2 } .$

With a little algebra we can combine coeffi-
cients of the same predictor to rewrite this
hazard model as shown here.

Thus, for $\mathrm { g } > 1 ,$ if $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ is a $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$ variable, the other
$\mathrm { X s }$ are covariates, and there are no product
terms $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ in the model, the formula for the
☒ ☒
HR for the effect of $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ adjusted for the covari-
ates is $\exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right] .$ This $\mathrm { H R }$ expression would
again need to be modified if the model contains
$p r o d u c t$ terms of the form $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } .$
☒


### EXAMPLE OF LM MODEL FOR BYAR DATA

Separate models approach (Method 1):
Cause-specific model: Cancer
CVD and Other deaths censored

No-interaction model

$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { C a } } \left( t , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { a } } \left( t \right) \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { a } } \mathrm { R x } \right.$$
$$+ \beta _ { 2 } { } _ { \mathrm { C a } } \mathrm { A g e } + \beta _ { 3 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { a } } \mathrm { W } t +$$
$$\beta _ { 4 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { a } } \mathrm { P F } + \beta _ { 5 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { a } } \mathrm { H x } + \beta _ { 6 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { a } } \mathrm { H } \mathrm { G }$$
$$\left. + \beta _ { 7 \mathrm { C a } } \mathrm { S Z } + \beta _ { 8 \mathrm { C a } } \mathrm { S G } \right]$$
$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C a } } \left( \mathrm { R X } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { R X } = 0 \right) = \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 \mathrm { C a } } \right]$$

We now illustrate the above general LM model
formation using the Byar data.

Recall that using Method 1, the separate models
approach, the Cox hazard formula used to fit a
separate model for Cancer deaths, treating CVD
and Other deaths as censored is repeated here.

Also shown is the formula for the hazard ratio
for the effect of the Rx variable, adjusted for
other variables in the model.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: VIII. Method 2-The Lunn-McNeil (LM) Approach 459" -->

LM SC Model for Byar Data

$$g = 1 , 2 , 3$$
$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } \left( t \right)$$ ☒

$\times \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { R X } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \beta _ { 8 } \mathrm { S G } \right.$
$+ \delta _ { 2 1 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R x } + \delta _ { 2 2 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \delta _ { 2 8 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S G }$
$\left. + \delta _ { 3 1 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { R x } + \delta _ { 3 2 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \delta _ { 3 8 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { S G } \right]$

1st row: predictors

Rx, Age, Wt, PF, ... , SG
2nd row: products

D2Rx, $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S G }$
3rd row: products
$D _ { 3 } R x ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { S G }$
$\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } = \mathrm { C V D }$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 } = \mathrm { O T H }$ are $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$
dummy variables that distinguish
the 3 event-types

HRCa(Rx = 1 vs. $\left. \mathrm { R x } = 0 \right) = \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \right]$

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C V D } } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { R x } = 0 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left[ \left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 2 1 } \right) \right]$$
$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { O T H } } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { R x } = 0 \right)$$

$= \exp \left[ \left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 3 1 } \right) \right]$

log likelihood = - 1831.92


Using the general LM data layout given in
Table 9.11, the stratified Cox LM model for
the Byar data that incorporates $C = 3$ event-
types is shown at the left. The strata, denoted
by $g = 1 , 2 , 3 ,$ identify the three event-types as
Cancer, CVD, and Other, respectively.

Notice that in the exponential part of the model,
there are 3 rows of terms that correspond to the
3 event-types of interest. The first row contains
$p = 8$ predictors $R x ,$ Age, Wt, PF, HX, HG, $S Z ,$
SG. The second row contains product terms of
the dummy variable $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ (the CVD indicator)
with each of the 8 predictors. Similarly, the
third row contains product terms of $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 }$ (the
Other indicator) with each of the predictors.

From the above model, it follows that the haz-
ard ratio formulas for the effects of Rx corres-
ponding to each event-type are as shown at the
left. Notice that for CVD and Other deaths,
the coefficient $\delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } }$ of the product term $D _ { g } R x ,$
$g = 2 , 3 ,$ is added to the coefficient $\beta _ { 1 }$ of $\mathrm { R x }$ in
the exponential term.

Table 9.14 shows edited output obtained from
fitting the above LM model.

The first eight rows of output in this table are
identical to the corresponding eight rows of out-
put in the previously shown Table 9.1 obtained
from Method 1, which fits a separate model for
Cancer deaths only. This equivalence results
because the first eight rows of the LM output
correspond to the reduced version of the LM
model when $D _ { 2 } = D _ { 3 } = 0 ,$ which identifies
Cancer as the event of interest.

However, the remaining 16 rows of LM output
are not identical to the corresponding 8 rows
of Table 9.2 (for CVD) and 8 rows of Table 9.3
(for Other). Note that the remaining 16 coeffi-
cients in the LM output identify the $\delta _ { \mathrm { g i } }$ co-
efficients in the LM model rather than the
sum $\left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g j } } \right)$ required for computing the HR
when $\mathrm { g } = 2$ and 3.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="460 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

$$\widehat { H R } _ { \mathrm { C a } } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { R x } = 0 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left[ - 0 . 5 5 0 \right] = 0 . 5 7 7$$
$$= \left( 1 / 1 . 7 3 3 \right)$$

Wald $\mathrm { C h i S q } = \left( - . 5 5 0 / . 1 7 1 \right) ^ { 2 }$

$$= 1 0 . 3 4 5 \left( P = 0 . 0 0 1 \right)$$

95% CI for $\exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 \mathrm { C a } } \right] :$
$\exp \left[ - 0 . 5 5 0 \pm 1 . 9 6 \left( 0 . 1 7 1 \right) \right]$
$= \left( 0 . 4 1 3 , 0 . 8 0 7 \right)$

LM results for Cancer identical to
Method 1 results for Cancer

$$\widehat { H R } _ { C V D } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s . } \mathrm { R x } = 0 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left( \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { 1 1 } \right)$$
$$= \exp \left( - 0 . 5 5 0 + 0 . 9 0 5 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left( 0 . 3 5 5 \right) = 1 . 4 2 6$$
$$\widehat { H R } _ { O T H } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s . } \mathrm { R x } = 0 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left( \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { 2 1 } \right)$$
$$= \exp \left( - 0 . 5 5 0 - 0 . 0 2 8 \right)$$
$$= \exp \left( - 0 . 5 7 8 \right) = 0 . 5 6 1$$

LM results for CVD and Other
identical to Method 1 results for
CVD and Other

Wald test statistics for CVD $a n d$
Other

$$\mathrm { W a l d } _ { \mathrm { C V D } } = \left[ \frac { \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { 1 1 } } { \mathrm { S E } _ { \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { 2 1 } } } \right] ^ { 2 }$$
$$\mathrm { W a l d } _ { \mathrm { O T H } } = \left[ \frac { \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { 1 1 } } { \mathrm { S E } _ { \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { 2 1 } } } \right] ^ { 2 }$$

Computer packages provide for
computation of the above formulas

SAS: Contrast statement

STATA: lincom command

From Table 9.14, the adjusted $\widehat { H R }$ for the effect
of $\mathrm { R x }$ when the event-type is Cancer can be
read directly off the output as 0.577. Also, the
Wald statistic for testing $\mathrm { H } _ { 0 } :$ $\beta _ { 1 } = 0$ is highly
significant $\left( \mathrm { P } = . 0 0 1 \right) .$ The corresponding 95%
confidence interval for this HR has the limits
$\left( 0 . 4 1 3 , 0 . 8 0 7 \right) .$

These results are identical to those obtained for
the adjusted $\widetilde { H R } ,$ the Wald test, and interval
estimate obtained in Table 9.1 using Method 1
to assess the effect of $R x$ on survival for cancer
death.

Using Table 9.14 to obtain adjusted $\widehat { H R }$ for the
$R x$ effect when the event-type is CVD or Other,
we must exponentiate the sum $\left( \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right)$ for
$\mathrm { g } = 2$ and 3, respectively.

These results are shown at the left, and they
are identical to those obtained in Tables 9.2
and 9.3 using Method 1.

Note, however, that using the LM model to
obtain Wald test statistics and 95% confidence
intervals for the HRs for CVD and Other, the
mathematical formulas (shown at left for the
Wald tests) require obtaining standard errors
of the sums $\left( \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { g 1 } \right)$ for $\mathrm { g } = 2$ and 3, whereas
the output in Table 9.14 gives only individual
standard errors of $\widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } ,$ $\widehat { \delta } _ { 1 1 }$ and $\widehat { \delta } _ { 2 1 } .$

SAS ans STATA provide special syntax to spec-
ify the computer code for such computations:
SAS's PHREG allows a "Contrast" statement;
STATA allows a "lincom" command.

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: IX. Method 2a: Alternative Lunn-McNeil (LMalt) Approach 461" -->

## IX. Method 2a: Alternative Lunn-McNeil $\left( L M _ { a l t } \right)$ Approach

· Uses same data layout as
Table 9.12

· Column headings:

· Dummy variables
$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } }$

· Predictor variables
$\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$

· Above variables are
transformed into product
terms


### 1st row of $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model: product terms

$D _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } , D _ { 1 } X _ { 2 } , \ldots , D _ { 1 } X _ { p }$
coefficients $\delta ^ { \prime } { } _ { 1 1 } , \ldots , \delta ^ { \prime } { } _ { 1 \mathrm { p } }$

1st row of LM model
predictors $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$
coefficients $\beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { \mathrm { r } }$

General Stratified Cox
$\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ Model

$$\mathrm { g } = 1 , \ldots , \mathrm { C }$$
$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { g } } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } ^ { \prime } \left( t \right)$$
$$\times \exp \left[ \delta _ { 1 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + 8 _ { 1 2 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { 1 \mathrm { p } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right.$$
$$+ 8 _ { 2 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \mathfrak{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + 8 _ { 2 \mathrm { p } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$$
$$+ \delta _ { 3 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 3 2 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { 3 \mathrm { p } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$$
$$+ \cdots .$$
$$\left. + \delta _ { \mathrm { C l } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { C 2 } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { \mathrm { C p } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$$

The data layout required to fit the $L M _ { a l t }$ model
is the same as shown earlier in Table 9.12.
However, the variables listed in the columns
of this table, namely, the dummy variables $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } ,$
$\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ and the predictor variables $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$
$\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } ,$ serve as basic variables that are trans-
formed into product terms that define the
$L M _ { a l t } m o d e l .$

The primary difference in the two formulas
is that the first row of the exponential term in
the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model contains product terms $D _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } ,$
$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } }$ with coefficients denoted
$8 ^ { \prime } 1 1 1 \cdots . . 8 ^ { \prime } 1 p$ whereas the first row in the $\mathrm { L M }$
model contains the predictors $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { X } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { p }$
without product terms and coefficients
denoted $\beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { \mathrm { p } } .$

The general form of the LMalt model is shown
at the left. We have used a superscript prime (')
to distinguish the hazard model formula for
the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model from the corresponding
formula for the $L M$ model given earlier.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="462 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

. $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ and LM models are
different

· Estimated regression
coefficients will not be identical

· Estimated HRs, test statistics,
and interval estimates are
identical

· Computational formulas are
different


### LMalt Hazard Model for Event-Type 1

$$\mathrm { h } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$$ ☒

$\times \exp \left[ \delta _ { 1 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 1 2 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { 1 \mathrm { p } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$
☒ ☒

$\left( \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } = 1 , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } = \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } = \cdots = \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } = 0 \right)$
$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { g } = 1 } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 0 \right) = \exp \left[ \delta _ { 1 1 } ^ { \prime } \right]$
☒ ☒

(no $X _ { j } X _ { i }$
☒ ☒

$\mathrm { L M } \mathrm { H R } = \mathrm { e x p } \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \right]$

$L M _ { a l t }$ Hazard Model for
$E v e n t - T y p e \quad g \left( > 1 \right)$

$$\mathrm { h } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } ^ { \prime } \left( t \right)$$ ☒

$$\times \exp \left[ \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 2 } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { \mathrm { g p } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right]$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

$\left( D _ { g } = 1 \text { and } D _ { g ^ { \prime } } = 0 \text { for } g ^ { \prime } \neq g \right)$
$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { g } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } = 0 \right) = \exp \left[ \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } ^ { \prime } \right]$
☒ ☒

(no products $X _ { j } X _ { 1 }$ in model)
☒

$\mathrm { L M } \mathrm { H R } = \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right]$

Because the LMalt model and the LM model are
different hazard models, their estimated
regression coefficients will not be identical.
Nevertheless, when used on the same dataset,
the estimated HRs of interest and their
corresponding test statistics and interval esti-
mates are identical even though the formulas
used to compute these statistics are different
for the two models.

For $g = 1$ (i.e., event-type 1), the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model
simplifies to the expression shown at the left.
Note that because $g = 1 ,$ the values of the
dummy variables are $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ and $D _ { 2 } = D _ { 3 } =$
$\ldots = \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } = 0 .$

Thus, for $g = 1 ,$ if $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ is a $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$ variable, the
☒
other Xs are covariates, and there are no prod-
uct terms of the form $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ in the model, the
☒
formula for the HR for the effect of $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ adjusted
☒
for the covariates is $\exp \left[ \delta _ { 1 1 } ^ { \prime } \right] .$

Recall that for the LM model, the correspon-
ding HR formula also involved the coefficient
of the $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ variable, denoted as $\beta _ { 1 } .$

For any $\mathrm { g }$ greater than 1, the general hazard
model simplifies to a hazard function formula
that contains only those product terms involv-
ing the subscript $\mathrm { g } ,$ because $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { g } } = 1$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { g } ^ { \prime } } = 0$
for $\mathrm { g } ^ { \prime } \neq \mathrm { g } .$

Thus, for $\mathrm { g } > 1 ,$ if $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ is a $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$ variable, the
☒
other Xs are covariates, and there are no pro-
ducts $\mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 }$ in model, the formula for the HR
☒
for the effect of $X _ { 1 }$ adjusted for the covariates is
$\exp \left[ \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } ^ { \prime } \right] .$
☒

Recall that for the LM model, the exponential
in the HR formula involved the sum $\left( \beta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right) .$

<table>
<tr>
<td>Statistical inferences (i.e., Wald test, 95% CI)</td>
<td rowspan="2">Thus for $\mathrm { g } > 1 ,$ statistical inferences about HRs using the $L M _ { a l t }$ model only require use of the standard error for $\widehat { \delta } _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } ^ { \prime }$ that is directly provided in the output.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model: need standard error for $\widehat { \delta } _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } ^ { \prime }$ (directly provided by output)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>LM model: standard error of $\left( \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right) .$ (more complicated computation)</td>

<td>$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { g } } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$$ $$\times \exp \left[ \delta _ { 1 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { R x } + \cdots + \delta _ { 1 8 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { S G } \right.$$ $$+ \delta _ { 2 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R x } + \cdots + \delta _ { 2 8 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S G }$$ $$\left. + \delta _ { 3 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { R x } + \cdots + \delta _ { 3 8 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { S G } \right]$$ ☒</td>

<td rowspan="2">Notice that in the exponential part of the model, the first row contains product terms of the dummy variable $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ (the CA indicator) with each of the 8 predictors Rx, Age, Wt, PF, HX, HG, SZ, SG. Recall that in the $L M$ version of this model, the first row contained main effects of the predictors instead of product terms. The second and third rows, as in the LM model, contain product terms of the dummy variable $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ (the CVD indicator) and $\mathrm { D } _ { 3 }$ (the OTH indicator), respectively, with each of the 8 - predictors.</td>
</tr>

<td>1st row: products $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \mathrm { R x } ,$ D1Age, ... , DISG (LM predictors, $\left. \mathrm { R x } , \mathrm { A g e } , \ldots , \mathrm { S G } \right)$ 2nd row: products D2Rx, $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S G }$ 3rd row: products D3Rx, $D _ { 3 } \text { Age } , \ldots , D _ { 3 } S G$</td>

<td>From the above model, it follows that the HR formulas for the effects of $\mathrm { R x }$ corresponding to each event-type are of the form $\exp \left( \delta _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ where $\delta _ { \underline { \mathrm { g } } 1 } ^ { \prime }$ the coefficient of the product term $D _ { g } R x$ in the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } } \mathrm { m o d e l } .$</td>

Tables 9.1, 9.2, 9.3 (Method 1)
output combined

Consequently, Wald test statistics (shown at
the left) and confidence intervals for these
HRs use standard errors that are directly
obtained from the standard error column
from the output obtained for the LMalt model.

Thus, the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model allows the user to per-
form statistical inference procedures using the
information directly provided in the computer
output, whereas the LM model requires addi-
tional computer code to carry out more com-
plicated computations.

Table 9.15 shows edited output obtained from
fitting the above $L M _ { a H }$ model.

The first eight rows of output in this table are
identical to the eight rows of output in the
previously shown Table 9.1 obtained from
Method 1, which fits a separate model for
Cancer deaths only, treating CVD and Other
deaths as censored.

The next eight rows in the table are identical
to the eight rows of output in the previous
Table 9.2, which fits a separate model for
CVD deaths only, treating Cancer and Other
deaths as censored.

The last eight rows in the table are identical
to the eight rows of output in the previous
Table 9.3, which fits a separate model for
Other deaths only, treating Cancer and CVD
deaths as censored.

Thus, the output in Table 9.15 using the single
$L M _ { a l t }$ model gives identical results to what is
obtained from fitting 3 separate models in
Tables 9.1, 9.2, and 9.3.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## Presentation: X. Method 1 (Separate Models) versus Method 2 (LM Approach) 465

X. Method 1 (Separate
Models) versus
Method 2
(LM Approach)

Why bother with LM or $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$
models when you can simply fit
3 separate models?

Answer: Can perform statistical
inferences that cannot be done
when fitting 3 separate models

LM Model for Byar Data

$$g = 1 , 2 , 3$$
$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t \right)$$
$$\times \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { R X } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \beta _ { 8 } \mathrm { S G } \right.$$
$$+ \delta _ { 2 1 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R x } + \delta _ { 2 2 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \delta _ { 2 8 } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S G }$$
$$\left. + \delta _ { 3 1 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { R x } + \delta _ { 3 2 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \delta _ { 3 8 } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { S G } \right]$$

The reader may have the following question at
this point: Why bother with the $L M$ or $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$
models as long as you can get the same results
from fitting three separate models using
Method 1? The answer is that the LM or $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$
model formulation allows for performing
statistical inferences about various features of
the competing risk models that cannot be con-
veniently assessed when fitting three separate
models using Method 1.

We illustrate such "extra" inference-making
using the LM model previously described for
the Byar data example. This model is shown
again at the left. Equivalent inferences can be
made using the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ model (see Exercises at
end of this chapter).

Inference question: Byar data

No-interaction SC LM model
versus
interaction SC LM model

No-interaction SC model

$$g = 1 , 2 , 3$$
$$\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t \right)$$

$\times \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { R X } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \beta _ { 8 } \mathrm { S G } \right]$

Assumes

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C A } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \right) = \mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C V D } } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \right)$$
$$= H R _ { O T H } \left( X _ { i } \right)$$
$$\equiv \mathrm { H R } \left( \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒
for any $X _ { i }$ variable
☒

for example, $\mathrm { R x } = 0$ vs $\mathrm { R x } = 1$ 1:

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C A } } \left( \mathrm { R x } \right) = \mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C V D } } \left( \mathrm { R x } \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { O T H } } \left( \mathrm { R x } \right)$$
$$= \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \right]$$

☒

One inference question to consider for the Byar
data is whether a no-interaction SC LM model
is more appropriate than the interaction SC
LM model defined above.

The no-interaction SC model is shown here at
the left.

This model assumes that the hazard ratio for
the effect of a single predictor (say, binary) $X _ { 1 }$
adjusted for the other variables in the model is
the same for each event-type of interest.

For example, in the above no-interaction SC
LM model the hazard ratio for the effect of Rx
is $\exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \right]$ for each $\mathrm { g } ,$ where $\beta _ { 1 }$ is the coefficient
of Rx.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="466 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

$\mathrm { H } _ { 0 } :$ all $\delta _ { \mathrm { g j } } = 0 ,$

$g = 2 , 3 ; j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , 8$

where $\delta _ { \mathrm { g i } }$ is coefficient of $D _ { g } X _ { j }$ in
☒
the interaction SC LM model

Likelihood Ratio Test

$L R = - 2 \log L _ { R } - \left( - 2 \log L _ { F } \right)$
approx x16 under $\mathrm { H } _ { 0 }$
$R = n o$ interaction SC (reduced)
model
F = interaction SC (full) model

Table 9.16: Log likelihoodR
$= - \quad 1 8 9 2 . 0 9 1$
Table 9.14: Log likelihoodF
$= - \quad 1 8 3 1 . 9 1 6$

$$L R = - 2 \log L _ { R } - \left( - 2 \log L _ { F } \right)$$
$$= - \quad 2 \left( - 1 8 9 2 . 0 9 1 \right)$$
$$- \left( - 2 \left( - 1 8 3 1 . 9 1 6 \right) \right)$$
$$= 1 2 0 . 3 5 \text { approx } \chi _ { 1 6 } ^ { 2 } \text { under Ho }$$
$$\left( \mathrm { P } < 0 . 0 0 1 \right)$$

Reject $\mathrm { H } _ { 0 } :$ interaction SC model
more appropriate than
no-interaction SC model

To carry out the comparison of the interaction
with the no-interaction SC LM models, the null
hypothesis being tested is that the coefficients
of the 16 product terms $\left( \delta _ { \mathrm { g j } } \right)$ in the interaction
SC model are equal to zero.

This null hypothesis is conveniently tested
using the $\mathrm { L M }$ model with a likelihood ratio
test statistic obtained by subtracting -2 log L
statistics from the two models being compared.
The degrees of freedom being tested is 16, the
number of $\delta _ { \mathrm { g j } }$ coefficients being set equal to
zero under $\mathrm { H } _ { 0 } .$

Table 9.16 gives the output resulting from the
no-interaction SC LM model for the Byar data-
set. In this table, there is one coefficient
corresponding to each of the eight predictors
in the model, as should be the case for a no-
interaction SC model. Nevertheless, baseline
hazard functions $\mathrm { h } _ { 0 \mathrm { g } } ^ { * } \left( t \right)$ are allowed to be differ-
ent for different $\mathrm { g }$ even if the coefficients are
the same for different $\mathrm { g } .$

From Table 9.16, we find that the log-likelihood
statistic for the reduced (no-interaction SC)
model is -1892.091. From Table 9.14 (or 9.15),
the log-likelihood statistic for the full (interac-
tion SC) model is -1831.916.

The likelihood ratio test statistic (LR) is then
calculated to be 120.35, as shown at the left.
This statistic has an approximate chi-square dis-
tribution with 16 degrees of freedom under $\mathrm { H } _ { 0 } .$

The P-value is less than .001, which indicates a
highly significant test result, thus supporting
use of the full-interaction SC model.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## Presentation: X. Method 1 (Separate Models) versus Method 2 (LM Approach) 467


## Cancer and CVD very different clinically

$$\mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C a } } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . 0 \right)$$

\+
$\neq \mathrm { H R } _ { \mathrm { C V D } } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 1 \mathrm { v s } . 0 \right)$


### DIFFERENT STUDY EXAMPLE

Competing risks: Stage 1 vs. Stage 2
Breast Cancer
$\Downarrow$

$H R _ { s t g 1 } \left( R x = 0 \text { vs. } 1 \right)$
$= \mathrm { H } \widetilde { \mathrm { R } } _ { \mathrm { s t g 2 } } \left( \mathrm { R x } = 0 \mathrm { v s } . 1 \right)$
11
No-interaction SC Cox reasonable
depending on similarity of competing
risks

For the Byar dataset, the decision to reject the
no-interaction SC model makes sense when
considering that two of the competing risks
are Cancer deaths and CVD deaths. Because
Cancer and CVD are clinically very different
diseases, one would expect the effect of any of
the predictors, particularly $\mathrm { R x } ,$ on time to failure
to be different for these different disease entities.

Suppose, however, the competing risks for a
different study had been, say, two stages of
breast cancer. Then it is plausible that the
effect from comparing two treatment regimens
might be the same for each stage. That is, a no-
interaction SC LM model may be (clinically)
reasonable depending on the (clinical) similar-
ity between competing risks.


### Unstratified LM model $\left( \mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { U } } \right) :$

$$\mathrm { h } ^ { * } \left( t , \mathrm { X } \right) = \mathrm { h } _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( t \right)$$
$$\times \exp \left[ \gamma _ { 1 } \mathrm { C V D } + \gamma _ { 2 } \mathrm { O T H } \right.$$
$$+ \beta _ { 1 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { R x } + \beta _ { 2 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \beta _ { 8 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { S G }$$ ☒

$+ \delta _ { 2 1 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R x } + \delta _ { 2 2 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \delta _ { 2 8 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S G }$
$+ \delta _ { 3 1 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { R x } + \delta _ { 3 2 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A g e } + \cdots + \delta _ { 3 8 } ^ { \bullet } \mathrm { D } _ { 3 } \mathrm { S G }$


## LMu model: CVD and OTH included in model

LM model: CVD and OTH not
included in model
(Both LMu and LM models use
augmented dataset)

$\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { U } }$ model: need to check PH
assumption (Chapter 4)
PH assumption not satisfied
17

Use LM instead of $\mathrm { L M } _ { U }$ model

Returning again to the Byar data example,
another variation of the LM model is shown
at the left and denoted $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { U } } .$ This is a Cox PH
model applied to the augmented data of Table
9.12 that is not stratified on the competing
risks (i.e., there is no subscript $g$ in the model
definition). We have used a superscript bullet
(.) to distinguish the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { U } }$ model from the LM
☒
and $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { a l t } }$ models.

The $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { U } }$ model includes the two event-type
dummy variables CVD and OTH in the
model, rather than stratifying on these vari-
ables. As for the LM model, the fit of the $\mathrm { L M } _ { \mathrm { U } }$
model is based on the augmented dataset given
in Table 9.12.

Because the $\mathrm { L M } _ { l }$ model is an unstratified Cox
PH model, we would want to use the methods
of Chapter 4 to assess whether the PH assump-
tion is satisfied for the CVD and OTH variables
(as well as the other variables). If the PH
assumption is found wanting, then the (strati-
fied Cox) LM model should be used instead.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="468 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

## XI. Summary


### Competing Risks

Each subject can experience only
one of several different types of
events over follow-up


### Typical approach

. Cox PH model

· Separate model for each event-
type

· Other (competing) event-types
treated as censored

This chapter has considered survival data in
which each subject can experience only one of
several different types of events over follow-up.
The different events are called competing risks.

We have described how to model competing risks
survival data using a Cox PH model. The typical
approach for analyzing competing risks data is
to perform a survival analysis for each event-type
separately, where the other (competing) event-
types are treated as censored categories.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: XI. Summary 469" -->


## Drawbacks

1\. Require independent competing
risks that is, censored subjects
have same risk as non-censored
subjects in risk set

2\. Product-limit (e.g., KM) curve
has questionable interpretation

Several
alternative
strategies
regarding independence assump-
tion: No single strategy is always best

Sensitivity analysis: worst-case vio-
lations of independence assumption

For example, subjects censored
from competing risks treated in
analysis as if

· All event-free

· All experience event of interest

· Independence assumption not
easily verifiable

· Typical analysis assumes
independence assumption is
satisfied


### CIC Alternative to KM

· Derived from cause-specific
hazard function

· Estimates marginal
probability when competing
risks are present

· Does not require independence
assumption

· Useful to assess treatment
utility in cost-effectiveness
analyses

There are two primary drawbacks to the above
method. One problem is the requirement that
competing risks be independent. This assump-
tion will not be satisfied if those subjects cen-
sored from competing risks do not have the
same risk for failing as subjects who are not
censored from the cause-specific event of inter-
est at that same time.

A second drawback is that the estimated
product-limit survival curve obtained from fit-
ting separate Cox models for each event-type
has questionable interpretation when there are
competing risks.

Regarding the independence assumption,
several alternative strategies for addressing
this issue are described, although no single
strategy is always best.

A popular strategy is a sensitivity analysis,
which allows the estimation of parameters by
considering worst-case violations of the inde-
pendence assumption. For example, subjects
censored from competing risks might be trea-
ted in the analysis as either all being event-free
or all experiencing the event of interest.

Unfortunately, the independence assumption
is not easily verifiable. Consequently, the typi-
cal competing risks analysis assumes that the
independence assumption is satisfied even if
this is not the case.

To avoid a questionable interpretation of the
KM survival curve, the primary alternative to
using KM is the Cumulative Incidence Curve
(CIC), which estimates the marginal probabil-
ity of an event. Marginal probabilities are rele-
vant for assessing treatment utility whether
competing risks are independent.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="470 9. Competing Risks Survival Analysis" -->

$$\mathrm { C I C } \left( t _ { \left( \mathrm { f } \right) } \right) = \sum _ { f ^ { \prime } = 1 } ^ { f } \widehat { I } _ { c } \left( t _ { \mathrm { f } ^ { \prime } } \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { f ^ { \prime } = 1 } ^ { f } \widehat { S } \left( t _ { f ^ { \prime } - 1 } \right) \widehat { h } _ { c } \left( t _ { f ^ { \prime } } \right)$$
$$\widehat { \mathrm { h } } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( \mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { f } } \right) = \mathrm { e s t i m a t e d } \mathrm { h a z a r d } \mathrm { a t } \mathrm { o r d e r e d }$$

The formula for the calculating the CIC is shown
at the left. The $\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } } \left( \mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { f } } \right)$ in the formula is the
estimated hazard at survival time $t _ { \mathrm { f } }$ for the
event-type (c) of interest. The term $S \left( t _ { f - 1 } \right)$
denotes the overall survival probability of previ-
ous time $\left( t _ { f - 1 } \right) ,$ where "overall survival" indicates
a subject that survives all competing events.

failure time $t _ { \mathrm { f } }$ for the event-type (c)

$S \left( t _ { f - 1 } \right) =$ = overall survival probabil-
ity of previous time $\left( t _ { \mathrm { f } - 1 } \right)$


## CIC

· Does not use product limit
formulation

· Not included in mainstream
commercially available
statistical packages (e.g., SAS,
STATA, SPSS, R)


### PH model used to obtain CIC

\+
Independence of competing risks
required

Modeling CIC with covariates using
PH model: Fine and Gray (1999)

Software available (Gebski, 1997)
Fine and Gray model analogous to
Cox PH model


## Alternative to CIC

$$\mathrm { C P C } _ { c } = \mathrm { P r } \left( \mathrm { T } _ { c } \leq t | \mathrm { T } \geq t \right)$$

where $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { c } }$ = time until event $c$
occurs

any
$$\mathrm { T } = \text { time until }$$
$$c o m p e t i n g \quad r i s k$$
$$\mathrm { C P C } _ { c } = \mathrm { C I C } _ { c } / \left( 1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { c ^ { \prime } } \right)$$
event
occurs

where $\mathrm { C I C } _ { c ^ { \prime } } = \mathrm { C I C }$ from risks other
than c

As the formula indicates, the CIC is not
estimated using a product-limit formulation.
Also, its computation is not included in main-
stream commercially available standard statisti-
cal packages.

If a proportional hazard model is used to
obtain hazard ratio estimates for individual
competing risks as an intermediate step in the
computation of a CIC, the assumption of inde-
pendent competing risks is still required.

Recent work of Fine and Gray (1999) provides
methodology for modeling the CIC with covari-
ates using a proportional hazards assumption.
Software is available for this method (Gebski,
1997, Tai et al., 2001), although not in standard
commercial packages.

An alternative to the CIC is the Conditional
Probability Curve (CPC). For risk type c,
$\mathrm { C P C } _ { c }$ is the probability of experiencing an
event c by time t, given that an individual has
not experienced any of the other competing risks
by time t.

The CPC can be computed from the CIC
through the formula $\mathrm { C P C } _ { c } = \mathrm { C I C } _ { c } / \left( 1 - \mathrm { C I C } _ { c ^ { \prime } } \right) ,$
where $\mathrm { C I C } _ { c }$ is the cumulative incidence of
failure from risks other than risk $c$ (i.e., all
other risks considered together).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Presentation: XI. Summary 471" -->

Tests to compare CPCs:

Pepe and Mori (1993) - 2 curves
Lunn (1998) - g curves


### Method 2: LM Approach

· Uses a single Cox (PH) model

· Gives identical results as
obtained from Method 1

· Allows flexibility to perform
statistical inferences not
available from Method 1

Pepe-Mori provide a test to compare two
CPC curves. Lunn (1998) extended this test to
g-groups and allows for strata.

We have also described an alternative approach,
called the Lunn-McNeil (LM) approach, for
analyzing competing risks data. The LM
approach allows only one model to be fit rather
than separate models for each event-type
(Method 1). This method is equivalent to using
Method 1. The LM model also allows the flexi-
bility to perform statistical inferences to deter-
mine whether a simpler version of an initial LM
model is more appropriate.

To carry out the LM approach, the data layout
must be augmented. If there are $\mathrm { C }$ competing
risks, the original data must be duplicated $\mathrm { C }$
times, one row for each failure type.

$\times \exp \left[ \beta _ { 1 } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \beta _ { 2 } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \beta _ { \mathrm { p } } \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { p } } \right.$
☒

$$+ \cdots .$$
$$\left. + \delta _ { \mathrm { C 1 } } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } + \delta _ { \mathrm { C 2 } } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { \mathrm { C p } } \mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { C } } \mathrm { X } _ { p } \right]$$

LM model: need standard error of
$\left( \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right)$

(special syntax required for com-
putation)

To use the LM approach with augmented data to
obtain identical results from fitting separate
models (Method 1), an interaction version of
a stratified Cox (SC) PH model is required.
A general form for this model is shown at the left.

The LM model can be used to obtain Wald test
statistics and 95% confidence intervals for
HRs separately for each competing risk. These
statistics require obtaining standard errors of
the sums of estimated regression coefficients
(e.g., $\left. \widehat { \beta } _ { 1 } + \widehat { \delta } _ { \mathrm { g 1 } } \right) .$ Such computations require
special syntax available in standard computer
packages such as SAS, STATA, SPSS, and $R .$

<!-- PageBreak -->
